Passage: Mohammed Abdul Hamid vs The State Rep. By on 2 December, 2022. IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT MADRAS DATED: 02.12.2022. CORAM: THE HONOURABLE MS JUSTICE R.N.MANJULA. Crl.O.P.No.29641 of 2022 Mohammed Abdul Hamid ...Petitioner Vs. 1.The State rep. by Inspector of Police, T-14 Mangadu Police, Chennai – 600 056. (Crime No.76 of 2021) 2.Salim ...Respondents Criminal Original Petition filed under Section 482 of the Criminal Procedure Code, to call for the records in Crime No.76 of 2021 on the file of the 1st respondent and quash the same. For Petitioners : Mr.K.Thenrajan For 1st Respondent : Mr.E.Raj Thilak Additional Public Prosecutor ORDER This Criminal Original Petition has been filed to call for the records in Crime No.76 of 2021 on the file of the 1st respondent and quash the same. On the complaint given by the 2nd respondent that his sister's daughter was missing from 12.12.2021, a case has been registered for girl missing in Crime No.76 of 2021 on the file of the T-14 Mangadu Police Station, Chennai 600 056 and subsequently the charges have been altered from girl missing to Sections 6 r/w 5J (ii) (i) POCSO Act, 2012. Today when the matter is taken up for hearing, it is submitted that the petitioner had married the victim girl as per Muslim rites and customs and the couple has a three months old infant born out of their marriage. It is further submitted that the de facto complainant who is the paternal uncle of the victim girl has also filed an affidavit by stating that in view of the marriage between the victim and the petitioner, he does not want to pursue the case. The petitioner, the defacto complainant, the victim girl and her mother were present before this Court and they were identified by the Police viz., Ms.K.Gracy, SSI, SRMC All Women Police Station, Mangadu. The 2nd respondent has produced the copy of the Aadhar Card. A joint compromise memo, dated 02.12.2022 has also been filed. The second respondent has stated that she affixed her signature without fear or coercion. The records would reveal that the date of birth of the victim girl is 03.06.2004 and she has attained majority. As it has been stated already, the marriage between the victim girl and the accused had taken place and they have constituted a family and become parents for a female child. In such circumstances, if the prosecution is allowed to continue, that will not serve any useful purpose and that will de-stabilize the family equilibrium of the petitioner and the victim. Even though the offences under POCSO Act are non-compoundable in nature, in the circumstances of the present case, I feel it is appropriate to take the cue from the principles laid down by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in the case of Parbatbhai Aahir Vs. State of Gujarat reported in AIR 2017 SC 4843. In the said case, in Paragraph 15, it has held as under: 15.The Broad Principles which emerge from the precedents on the subject, may be summarised in the following propositions: (i) Section 482 Cr.P.C preserves the inherent powers of the High Court to prevent an abuse of the process of any court or to secure the ends of justice. The provision does not confer new powers. It only recognises and preserves powers which inherent in the High Court. (ii) The invocation of the jurisdiction of the High Court to quash a first information report or a criminal proceeding on the ground that a settlement has been arrived at between the offender and the victim is not the same as the invocation of jurisdiction for the purpose of compounding an offence. While compounding an offence, the power of the court is governed by the provisions of Section 320 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973. The power to quash under Section 482 is attracted even if the offence is non-compoundable. The petitioner who had married the victim girl, if subjected to trial that would affect the interest of the victim girl herself. In order to serve the ends of justice, I feel it is appropriate to quash the criminal proceedings against the petitioner by invoking the powers of this Court under Section 482 of Cr.P.C. In the result, this Criminal Original Petition stands allowed and as a sequel, the further proceedings in Crime No.76 of 2021 on the file of the 1strespondent, is quashed in terms of compromise. The terms of Joint Compromise Memo, dated 02.12.2022 shall form part of this Order.
Question: Considering the court proceedings in the matter of Mohammed Abdul Hamid and the State, what legal implications arise when a victim, who has attained the age of majority, consents to marry their abuser, especially in the context of the POCSO Act? Should the previous allegations under the Act be dismissed based on subsequent circumstances such as marriage?
Correct Answer: Yes, as long as the victim is of legal age and consents, charges can be dismissed.
Reasoning: The passage illustrates that the victim girl has attained majority and consented to marry the petitioner. Given these circumstances, the court may decide to dismiss the POCSO charges based on the new family dynamics, as long as the marriage is valid and consensual.

Passage: IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA KALABURAGI BENCH DATED THIS THE 14TH DAY OF MARCH, 2017 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MR.JUSTICE B. A. PATIL CRIMINAL PETITION No.200301/2017 Between: 1. Basawaraj Nayak S/o Dyawappa Malladgud Age: 40 years, Occ: Agriculture R/o PWD Camp, Sirwar Village Tq. Manvi, Dist. Raichur 2. Smt. Laxmi W/o Basawaraj Nayak Age: 35 years, Occ: Household R/o PWD Camp, Sirwar village Tq. Manvi, Dist. Raichur ... Petitioners (By Sri Avinash A. Uploankar, Advocate for Sri Shivanand Patil, Advocate) And: The State through Manvi Police Station Represented by Addl. SPP High Court of Karnataka Kalaburagi Bench ... Respondent (By Sri P.S. Patil, HCGP) This Criminal Petition is filed under Section 439 of Cr.P.C., praying to enlarge the petitioners on bail in Spl. FIR (POCSO) 10/2017 pending on the file of Additional Sessions Judge, Raichur, which is registered for the offences punishable under Sections 376, 109, 504, 307, 506 r/w Section 34 of IPC and also for the offences punishable under Sections 4 and 6 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012. This petition is coming on for Orders this day, the Court made the following:- ORDER This petition is filed by the petitioners/accused Nos.2 and 3 under Section 439 of Cr.P.C., seeking regular bail in Crime No.22/2017 of Manvi Police Station, registered for the offences punishable under Sections 376, 109, 504, 307, 506 r/w Section 34 of IPC and also for the offences punishable under Sections 4 and 6 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 ('the Act' for short). Brief facts leading to filing of the complaint are that the victim has been brought up in the house of the complainant. Accused No.1/child-in-conflict with law used to visit the house of his sister. In that light, on 24.01.2017 at about 9.00 p.m., the complainant received information by the people of that area to the effect that accused No.1/child-in-conflict with law has outraged the modesty of the victim and she was screaming and immediately, she went to the house of Huligemma and through window she saw the child-in-conflict with law was undressed and was outraging the modesty of the victim. Despite shouting, he did not open the door and finally, he outraged the modesty of the victim and returned to home. Next day, when her husband came to home, the complainant narrated the incident to him and her husband called the petitioners over the phone and asked them to come over to the village to discuss about the incident. On 25.01.2017 at about 11.00 p.m., when the complainant was in the house, the accused persons came there by holding deadly weapons and started abusing her by saying that they wanted to grab the land belonging to the victim as such, they have got committed the said offence through accused No.1. They have assaulted on the head of the complainant with stick and assaulted by hands. On hearing the noise, neighbors came and rescued her. I have heard the learned counsel for the petitioners and the learned High Court Government Pleader appearing for respondent-State. The main grounds urged by Sri Avinash A. Uploankar, learned counsel appearing for Sri Shivanand Patil, learned counsel for the petitioners are that petitioners have been implicated in this case only with an intention to grab the property. The allegations made against the petitioners are that they have abetted accused No.1 to commit the said crime and they have assaulted the complainant on the next day when they went to question about the alleged incident. There are no overt-acts so far as the other offences which have been committed by accused No.1. He has also contended that the injured has been discharged from the hospital. He has further contended that petitioners have got both movable and immovable properties, therefore, there is no chance of they being absconded. He has also contended that if the petitioners are released on bail, they are ready to abide by the conditions to be imposed by this Court and they are ready to offer sureties. On these grounds, he prays for allowing the petition. On the contrary, learned High Court Government Pleader appearing for the respondent-State has vehemently contended that petitioners have abetted accused No.1 to commit serious offence of sexual assault against the victim. He has also contended that at this juncture, if the petitioners are released on bail, they may tamper with the prosecution witnesses, they may abscond and they may not be available for trial. On these grounds, he prays for dismissal of the petition. I have gone through the contents of the complaint and other material produced along with the petition. On going through the contents of the complaint, it would indicate that at the first instance the alleged incident has taken place on 24.01.2017 by accused No.1 who is considered to be child in conflict with law. He has been released on bail by the Juvenile Justice Board. The allegation made against the petitioners is that when they had been to the house of the complainant on 25.01.2017 at about 11.00 p.m., they have abused and assaulted the complainant with stick, hands and thereby, caused injuries. When the injured has been discharged from the hospital and when there are no serious allegations against the petitioners except the allegation of abetment, I feel that if the petitioners are released on bail by imposing stringent conditions, it would safeguard the interest of the prosecution and it would meet the ends of justice. For the aforementioned reasons, the petition is allowed and petitioners/accused Nos.2 and 3 are ordered to be released on bail, subject to the following conditions: i) The petitioners shall execute personal bonds for a sum of Rs.2,00,000/- (Rupees Two Lakhs Only) each with two solvent sureties each for the likesum to the satisfaction of the jurisdictional Court; ii) The petitioners shall not indulge in hampering the investigation or tampering the prosecution witnesses; iii) The petitioners shall make themselves available to the Investigating Officer as and when required; iv) The petitioners shall appear before the Trial Court regularly on all the dates of hearing without fail. v) The petitioners shall not leave the jurisdiction of the concerned Court without its prior permission.

Reasoning: Given the nature of the allegations and the potential threats posed to the victim by the accused, the passage and circumstances strongly suggest that immediate police intervention and protective measures are necessary to ensure the safety of the victim before considering bail for the accused.

Passage: This Criminal Miscellaneous Petition ... vs Judge on 21 February, 2022. This Criminal Miscellaneous Petition is filed to suspend the sentence imposed against the petitioner in Special S.C.No.5 of 2019, dated 21/02/2022 by the Sessions Judge, Special Court for Exclusive Trial of Cases under POCSO Act, Madurai and enlarge the petitioner on bail pending disposal of the criminal appeal. The case of the prosecution is that the petitioner called the victim girl aged about 9 years very often and sexually abused her by giving 10 rupees or 20 rupees for each occasion and thereafter, threatened the victim girl that in case if she inform the incident to her parents, he would cut her throat. On the basis of the complaint, FIR came to be registered in Crime No.9 of 2018 for the offence under sections 5(m),5(1) and 6 of the protection of Child From Sexual Offices Act, 2012 and subsequently, the case was altered into Sections 5(m), 5(1) and 6 of Protection of Child from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 and Section 506 (i) IPC. After completing investigation, the respondent police has laid the final report for the offences under sections 5(m), 5(1) and 6 of Protection of Child from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 and Section 506(i) IPC as against the petitioner/accused. To prove the prosecution case, 11 witnesses have been examined and 13 documents were marked, apart from that one Court exhibit as Ex.C1. On the side of the accused, one witness, but exhibit was marked. At the conclusion of trial, the trial Court found that the case against the accused was proved, convicted this petitioner and sentenced to undergo rigorous imprisonment for 20 years and to pay a fine of Rs.5,000/- with default clause for the offence under section 5(m), 5(1) and 5(i) r/w 6 of POCSO Act. Challenging the same, the accused has filed the appeal. Pending appeal, this miscellaneous petition is filed seeking suspension of sentence. Heard both sides. Perusal of the records shows that the appellant argued the main appeal before my predecessor and posted for respondent side argument. Now change of roster, the matter has to argue the main appeal, in the meantime, I find no specific ground made by the petitioner in this petition. Accordingly, this criminal miscellaneous petition dismissed. The appellant is directed to argue the main appeal afresh. Index:Yes/No Internet:Yes/No

Passage: Muthu Kumar vs The State on 31 January, 2025. This Criminal Appeal arises out of the judgment of conviction and sentence rendered by the learned Sessions Judge, Special Court for Exclusive Trial of Cases under the POSCO Act, Chennai, in Spl.S.C.No.195 of 2022, dated 31.10.2023. The appellant was tried along with one Bhuvaneswari (A2), the mother of the victim girl. The learned trial Judge found A2 guilty for the offence under Section 6 read with Section 17 @ 21(1) of the POCSO Act and convicted and sentenced her to undergo imprisonment for four months, and the period of detention already undergone has been set off under Section 428 Cr.P.C. The victim, P.W.2, who was born on 15.09.2004, was a minor at the time of the incident. She had been living with her mother (A2) since her father separated from the family, taking the victim's brother with him. The appellant, an auto driver, came into contact with the victim's family when he began providing transportation. Later, he informed the victim's mother that he had divorced his wife, and the appellant and A2 began living together with the victim girl. Taking advantage of the mother's absence, the appellant forcibly committed penetrative sexual assault on the victim girl. This abuse continued, and the victim later became pregnant. She gave birth to a male child at home, and when complications arose with the umbilical cord, the victim sought medical treatment. This led to the involvement of the Child Welfare Committee (CWC) and the Police, bringing the crime to light. Following medical treatment, the Primary Health Centre staff, Nammalwarpet reported the case to the CWC after discovering the victim's pregnancy and minor status. The CWC informed the Police, and thereafter, the case was registered in Crime No.11 of 2022 against the accused for the offences under Sections 5(j)(ii), 6 and 17 of the POCSO Act. During the investigation, the victim's statement was recorded. Subsequently, the victim was produced before a doctor and a Magistrate, where her statement under Section 164 of the Cr.P.C. was recorded. After recording statements from the witnesses and collecting the materials, the accused were arrested. A charge sheet was filed before the trial Court, listing 14 witnesses. Upon completion of the trial, the trial Court convicted the appellant (A1) under Section 6 of the POCSO Act. The second accused (A2) was convicted under Section 6 read with Section 17 @ 21 (1) of the POCSO Act. The learned counsel for the appellant contends that the trial Court's finding is contrary to both the law and the weight of the evidence. The learned counsel submits that the trial Court failed to properly consider the statement made by the victim, P.W.2, who categorically stated that she and the appellant had got married, and that a male child was born out of their relationship. According to P.W.2, she and the appellant were living together as husband and wife. P.W.2 further stated that their baby developed an infection in the umbilical cord, and the baby was taken to the Public Health Centre for treatment. Additionally, the learned Government Advocate (Criminal side) submits that the de-facto complainant in this case is a member of the Child Welfare Committee (CWC), who received information from the Public Health Centre in Nammalwarpet regarding the victim's condition. The trial Court correctly considered all the relevant materials and evidence in this case, including the victim's minor status, the DNA test report, and the statements recorded during the investigation. Based on this thorough and comprehensive analysis, the trial Court concluded that the appellant was guilty of the charges levelled against him. The victim hails from a marginalized background and lives in poverty. It is the appellant's family that takes care of the victim, supports her, and provides aid in all aspects. In the result, the criminal appeal is allowed; the conviction and sentences imposed on the appellant/A1 by judgment dated 31.10.2023, in Spl.S.C.No.195 of 2022, by the learned Sessions Judge, Special Court for Exclusive Trial of Cases under the POCSO Act, Chennai, is set aside and he is acquitted from all the charges.
Question: In the ongoing legal proceedings of Muthu Kumar vs The State, how essential is the role of the Child Welfare Committee (CWC) in handling cases of minors who are victims of alleged sexual offenses, such as in this case where the victim became pregnant? What legal standards or actions should the CWC be expected to adhere to for ensuring that the rights of the child are safeguarded and the legal proceedings are navigated effectively to protect the interests of the victim?
Correct Answer: The CWC needs to ensure that the privacy and psychological well-being of the victim are prioritized and that their actions are legally compliant and thorough.
Reasoning: The role of the CWC in protecting minors in cases of sexual offenses involves ensuring that the victim's rights are upheld, especially given her vulnerable status. The passage highlights that the CWC was involved following medical complications, indicating their duty to intervene and provide necessary support which must be thorough to ensure the investigation and the prosecution are aware of the victim's unique needs.

Passage:
Soni Kumari vs The State Of Bihar on 3 September, 2024

The present appeal has been preferred by the victim/informant and her father impugning the judgment dated 07.03.2019 passed by the learned Additional Sessions Judge-Ist-cum-Special Judge (POCSO Act), Sitamarhi in Trial No.25 of 2017, arising out of Sursand P.S. Case No. 129 of 2017, whereby respondent No. 2 viz., Sanjiv Kumar Singh has been acquitted of charges framed under Sections 376 and 506 of the Indian Penal Code and Sections 4 and 12 of the POCSO Act. The prosecution case as emerging from the written report of the victim/informant addressed to Officer-in-charge, Sursand Police Station, District-Sitamarhi is that the victim is aged about 14 years. On 12.05.2017 her father had gone to Sursand market and mother had gone to Anganwari Centre and she was alone at home. In the meantime, at 12 O'clock her neighbour Sanjiv Kumar Singh, who is respondent no.2 herein, entered into her courtyard and forcibly took her in room. When she raised voice, her mouth was closed by him by putting his hand and he started committing rape upon her. Despite efforts, she could not raise voice because her mouth was shut up by the respondent no.2 by putting his hand on her mouth. In the meantime, incidentally her father came and after hearing her crying, he entered into the house. Then he started fleeing away. But the victim and her father raised hulla and on their hulla, many people assembled there. Respondent no.2 made unsuccessful effort to flee away. He was apprehended by the people and taken to police station. On the basis of the written report, Sursand P.S. Case No. 129 of 2017 was registered against the sole accused, namely, Sanjiv Kumar Singh, for the offence punishable under Section 376 of the Indian Penal Code and Sections 4 and 12 of the POCSO Act, 2012.
Question: In light of the Soni Kumari vs The State of Bihar case, what are the key components that must be proven in a POCSO Act case for a conviction to be awarded? How does the burden of proof shift during the trial process for sexual offences against minors?
Correct Answer: The prosecution must prove both the age of the victim and that the offence occurred beyond reasonable doubt.
Reasoning: Under the POCSO Act, the prosecution bears the burden of proving the victim's age and the commission of the offence. If the prosecution fails to establish either of these foundational elements, the case against the accused cannot succeed.

Passage: IN THE HIGH COURT OF GUJARAT AT AHMEDABAD R/CRIMINAL MISC.APPLICATION NO. 19565 of 2021 KISHAN @ KISHLO KALUBHAI CHAROLA Versus STATE OF GUJARAT Appearance: MR HARNISH V DARJI(3705) for the Applicant(s) No. 1 for the Respondent(s) No. 2 MS.SHRUTI PATHAK APP(2) for the Respondent(s) No. 1 CORAM:HONOURABLE MR. JUSTICE ILESH J. VORA Date : 29/10/2021 ORAL ORDER [1] Mr.R.R.Joshi, learned advocate states that he has instructions to appear for and on behalf of respondent No.2 - original complainant. He is permitted to file his Vakaltnama. [2] Considering the issues involved in the present application and with consent of the learned advocates appearing for the respective parties as well as considering the fact that the dispute amongst the applicant and respondent No.2 has been resolved amicably, this application is taken up for final disposal forthwith. [3] Rule. Learned Additional Public Prosecutor as well as learned advocate appearing for the Complainant waive service of Rule on behalf of the respective respondents. [4] By way of this application under Section 482 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (hereinafter referred to as 'the Code'), the applicant has prayed for quashing and setting aside F.I.R. bearing C.R.No.I-12 of 2019 registered with Chalala Police Station, Dist. Amreli for the offence punishable under Sections363,376&366 of the Indian Penal Code, Sections 4&18 of the POCSO Act and to quash all other consequential proceedings arising out of the aforesaid FIR qua the applicant. [5] Heard learned advocates for the respective parties. [6] Learned advocate for the applicant submits that the applicant and victim have decided to live together as a husband and wife but due to some technicality of the age, the marriage could not be performed. Due to their relationship, a child is borne. He further submits that the applicant's family has also accepted the victim as daughter-in-law and her child and the the applicant's name is shown as father in the Birth Certificate issued by the Competent Authority. In support of his submissions, he has annexed Birth Certificate to the petition. [7] Both the learned advocates would submit that during the pendency of present application, the matter is amicably settled amongst the parties and therefore, any further continuation of the proceedings pursuant to the impugned FIR would create hardship to the parties and further continuation of the proceedings would amount to abuse of process of law. [8] Learned Additional Public Prosecutor appearing for the State has opposed the present application mainly on the ground that the victim is aged about 17 years and offence is serious in nature, therefore, complaint in question may not be quashed and the present application may be rejected. In support of her submissions, she relied upon the judgment in the case of The State of Madhya Pradesh Vs. Laxminarayan reported in (2019) 5 SCC 688. [9] Learned advocate for respondent No.2 has placed on record the settlement Affidavit duly sworn by the original complainant. The same is taken on record. The respondent No. 2 has filed affidavit stating inter-alia the fact that the matter is amicably settled with the applicant. The Respondent No.2 as well as victim are present in person before the Court and are identified by learned advocate for respondent No.2 and also admits the contents of the Affidavit. On inquiry made by the Court, the respondent No.2 has declared before this Court that the dispute between the applicant(s) and respondent No.2 is resolved and therefore, now the grievance stands redressed. It is therefore submitted that the present application may be allowed. [10] This Court has taken into consideration the fact that the victim is residing with the applicant and the parents of the applicant have also accepted as daughter-in-law and her child. The Birth Certificate of the Child is also on record which shows the applicant's name as father of the child. This Court has also taking into consideration the peculiar facts and circumstances of the case as well as facts of settlement and law laid down by the Apex Court [Gian Singh Vs. State of Punjab & Anr., reported in (2012) 10 SCC 303, Madan Mohan Abbot Vs. State of Punjab, reported in (2008) 4 SCC 582, Nikhil Merchant Vs. Central Bureau of Investigation & Anr., reported in 2009 (1) GLH 31, Manoj Sharma Vs. State & Ors., reported in 2009 (1) GLH 190 and Narinder Singh & Ors. Vs. State of Punjab & Anr. reported in 2014 (2) Crime 67 (SC),] this Court is of the considered view that further continuation of the criminal proceedings in relation to the impugned FIR would nothing but unnecessary harassment to the parties and trial thereon would be futile and further continuation of the proceedings would amount to abuse of process of law. Thus, to secure the ends of justice, the impugned FIR is required to be quashed and set aside in exercise of powers conferred under Section 482 of the Code. [11] Resultantly, this application is allowed and the impugned FIR bearing C.R.No.I-12 of 2019 registered with Chalala Police Station, Dist. Amreli filed against present applicant is hereby quashed and set aside and all other proceedings arising out of the aforesaid FIR are also quashed and set aside qua the applicant. Accordingly, Rule is made absolute. Direct service permitted.
Question: In the case of Kishan @ Kishlo Kalubhai Charola vs State Of Gujarat, what legal measures are available to a minor victim who has experienced a serious crime, in the context of this case, particularly focusing on the implications of the POCSO Act and the subsequent resolution of the case?
Correct Answer: The State must ensure appropriate legal actions under the POCSO Act regardless of the victim's wishes, as the law prioritizes protection.
Reasoning: Given that the victim is underage and the crime involves serious offenses under the POCSO Act, the State has an obligation to uphold the law to protect the minor, irrespective of any amicable resolution between the parties.

Passage: Xxxxx vs State Of Kerala on 3 March, 2025 IN THE HIGH COURT OF KERALA AT ERNAKULAM PRESENT THE HONOURABLE MR. JUSTICE RAJA VIJAYARAGHAVAN V & THE HONOURABLE MR.JUSTICE P. V. BALAKRISHNAN MONDAY, THE 3RD DAY OF MARCH 2025 / 12TH PHALGUNA, 1946 CRL.A NO. 572 OF 2021 CRIME NO.122/2017 OF Meenangadi Police Station, Wayanad AGAINST THE ORDER/JUDGMENT DATED 29.05.2019 IN SC NO.170 OF 2017 OF ADDITIONAL DISTRICT COURT & SESSIONS COURT - I, KALPETTA APPELLANT: XXXXX by ADV Rajendran T.G RESPONDENTS/STATE & COMPLAINANT: 1. STATE OF KERALA, REPRESENTED BY PUBLIC PROSECUTOR, HIGH COURT OF KERALA, ERNAKULAM - 682 031. 2. THE INSPECTOR OF POLICE, MEENANGADI POLICE STATION, WAYANAD DISTRICT - 673 591. This criminal appeal has been finally heard on 21.02.2025, the court on 3/3/2025 delivered the following judgment. This appeal is filed by the sole accused in SC No.170/2017, challenging his conviction under Sections 452, 376(2)(f),(n)&(i) and 506(ii) of IPC and Sections 5(l)&(n) r/w Section 6 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012('POCSO Act' for short) and sentence imposed under the aforementioned IPC provisions by the Special Court for trial of offences under POCSO Act and Children's Court, Kalpetta. The prosecution case is that on 5/3/2017 at about 10.15 pm, the accused, who is a close relative of the victim (PW2) aged 15 years, criminally trespassed into her house situated in Oorukandy Paniya Colony and took her to a shed nearby and committed rape/aggravated penetrative sexual assault upon her. Thereafter, on 15/3/2017 at about 12 am, inside the house of the accused situated in Mundavara, the accused after threatening and intimidating her, again committed rape/aggravated sexual assault upon her. In the trial court, from the side of the prosecution, PW1 to PW16 were examined and Exhibits P1 to P24 documents and MO1 and MO2 were marked. On examination under Section 313 Cr.P.C., the accused denied all the incriminating circumstances appearing against him in evidence and contended that he is innocent. From the side of the accused, no evidence was adduced. The trial court on an appreciation of the evidence on record, found the accused guilty and convicted him under Sections 452, 376(2)(f),(n)&(i)and Section 506(ii) of IPC and Sections 5(l)& (n) read with Section 6 of the POCSO Act. The accused was sentenced to undergo rigorous imprisonment for a period of five years and to pay a fine of Rs.10,000/- under Section 452 IPC and in default, to undergo RI for six months. The accused was also sentenced to undergo rigorous imprisonment for life for the remainder of his natural life and to pay a fine of Rs.50,000/- each for the offences under Sections 376(2)(n), 376(2)(i) and 376(2)(f) of IPC. In case of each default, the accused was ordered to undergo rigorous imprisonment for a period of one year each. The accused was further sentenced to undergo rigorous imprisonment for a period of five years and to pay a fine of Rs.10,000/- under Section 506 (ii) IPC. In case of default, the accused was ordered to undergo rigorous imprisonment for a period of six months. No separate sentence was awarded for the offences under the POCSO Act and the substantive sentences were ordered to run concurrently. While challenging the impugned judgment, the learned counsel for the appellant Adv. Tarin Rajendran contended that the evidence adduced by the prosecution, even if they are accepted in toto as gospel truth, will not establish the guilt of the accused.
Question: Based on the details provided in the passage, what legal remedies can be sought by a child victim of sexual assault like PW2 in the case discussed? How do these remedies align with the judicial proceedings and protections outlined in the passage?
Correct Answer: The victim can receive counseling services and should report to the police to initiate legal action against the perpetrator.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the victim, PW2, received support from her mother and subsequently reported the abuse to the police. It emphasizes the importance of legal action and the judicial process to ensure the victim's protection and rights, further supporting the need for counseling and legal remedies.

Passage: Hafij Ansari @ Chhotey vs State Of U.P. And Another on 7 November, 2019. HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT ALLAHABAD Case :- APPLICATION U/S 482 No. - 38130 of 2019 Applicant :- Hafij Ansari @ Chhotey Opposite Party :- State of U.P. and Another. The present application has been filed with a prayer to quash the entire proceedings of crime no. 33 of 2018, under Section 363, 366, 376 IPC and 3/4 POCSO Act, PS. Kubersthan, District Kushi Nagar. It has been argued by the learned counsel for the applicant that victim, who is daughter of the opposite party no. 2, has married accused-applicant and has child of six months born out of the wedlock. This aspect has not been taken into consideration by the Investigating Officer and has submitted charge sheet in this case in a routine manner which is nothing but an abuse of process and prosecution needs to be quashed. On the other hand, learned A.G.A. has drawn attention of the court to the statement of the victim under Section 164 Cr.P.C., in which, she has stated that she had fled from her house of her own free will and has married accused-applicant and she is pregnant of six months. In ossification test, her age is found to be 17 years and 16 years as per F.I.R., therefore, she was minor on the date of occurrence and was not competent to give consent for marriage, hence offence under Section 3/4 of POCSO Act as well as under Section 376 IPC would be made out in view of judgment of Apex Court rendered in Independent Thought vs. Union of India and another, (2017) 10 SCC 800, in which it is held that if a child is below 18 years, she would be treated to be child irrespective of the fact whether she is married or unmarried. If any intercourse is made with such child, the same would be fall in the category of rape, hence quashing should be refused.
Question: What potential consequences could arise for the accused in instances where the minor is declared a victim under the POCSO Act due to inappropriate circumstances surrounding marriage as highlighted in the court's reasoning?
Correct Answer: The accused may be subjected to criminal charges including those for rape and exploitation under the relevant child protection laws.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the court recognizes the implications of a minor's marriage within the context of the POCSO Act, establishing that engaging in sexual acts with a minor, regardless of her marital status, could lead to serious criminal implications for the accused.

The petitioner has filed the present application under Section 528 of the BNSS, 2023 r/w Section 482 of the Cr.P.C., 1973, to quash the proceedings where allegations of kidnapping a minor and subsequent marriage arose. The court must confront the complex interplay between the rights of the victim who seeks to marry the accused and the imperative of upholding the laws meant to protect minors. What legal principles guide the court's decisions when the victim aligns with social norms post-reconciliation, and how do these principles affect the justice system's response?
Question: How should the court approach cases involving the reconciliation of a victim who seeks to marry the accused, especially when serious allegations of crime against minors are involved? What legal standards govern such decisions while balancing victim autonomy and societal protection measures?
Correct Answer: The court should ensure that the victim's decision was made without coercion and that the relationship does not undermine protective measures for minors.
Reasoning: The passage highlights the principle that courts should assess the voluntary nature of the victim's choice to marry, alongside maintaining the protective intent of the POCSO Act. Legal standards governing these scenarios require a thorough evaluation to prevent potential exploitation while respecting the victim's autonomy.

It reflects the necessity for the prosecution to establish a clear connection between the alleged crime and the defendant using factual evidence as well as reliable testimonies. This illustrates the legal framework that prioritizes the protection of children while balancing the rights of the accused, emphasizing the forensic approaches required to substantiate claims in abusive contexts.

Passage: Chhaya Hazra vs Unknown on 7 February, 2025. This case involves an application for bail under Section 483 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023. The situation escalated when the Public Prosecutor noted that Md. Basar Ali, the District Child Protection Officer, was present but unwilling to speak during the trial about the children’s sexual abuse. The court ordered the Investigating Officer to investigate Ali's reluctance and criticized the Public Prosecutor for expunging Ali from the witness list and denying a forensic examination of vital evidence regarding the sexual exploitation of children in a home. The case was adjourned to 10th February, 2025, with a request for a report on the Public Prosecutor's conduct.
Question: What actions can the court take to ensure that all relevant evidence is thoroughly explored, particularly concerning the unwillingness of the District Child Protection Officer to testify, as stated in the case?
Correct Answer: The court could compel Md. Basar Ali to testify and ensure that all evidence, including forensic assessments, are reconsidered.
Reasoning: The passage highlights the significance of Md. Basar Ali's potential testimony and the need for a thorough investigation into all evidence related to the abuse. To uphold the victims' rights and the integrity of the judicial process, the court must ensure all relevant testimonies and forensic evidence are properly evaluated.

Passage: HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT ALLAHABAD Case :- CRIMINAL MISC. BAIL APPLICATION No. - 47044 of 2020 Applicant :- Tilak Singh @ Debu Opposite Party :- State of U.P. and Another Counsel for Applicant :- Raju Kanaujia Counsel for Opposite Party :- G.A. Hon'ble Rajeev Misra,J. Heard Mr. Raju Kanaujia, learned counsel for applicant, learned A.G.A. for State and perused the record. This application has been filed by the applicant Tilak Singh @ Debu seeking his enlargement on bail in Case Crime No.409 of 2019, under Sections 363, 366, 376(1) I.P.C. & 3/4 POCSO Act, Police Station- Gursahaiganj, District Kannauj during pendency of trial. Perusal of record shows that in respect of an occurrence which is alleged to have occurred on 8.8.2019, an F.I.R. dated 10.8.2019 was lodged by first informant Ganga Ram Fauji, which was registered as Case Crime No.409 of 2019, under Sections 363, 366, 376(1) I.P.C. & 3/4 POCSO Act, Police Station- Gursahaiganj, District Kannauj. In the aforesaid F.I.R., applicant Tilak Singh @ Debu has been nominated as solitary named accused. As per prosecution story as unfolded in the F.I.R., it is alleged that the daughter of the first informant has eloped with the applicant on account of his conduct. She has also taken away certain cash kept in the house of first informant. Learned counsel for applicant contends that the applicant and the prosecutrix are living together and from the cohabitation of the applicant and prosecutrix a child has been borne. Bail application has been supported by the affidavit of the prosecutrix herself. It is thus urged that present applicant is liable to be enlarged on bail. Per contra, the learned A.G.A. for the State has opposed the prayer for bail of the applicant, but could not dispute the aforesaid fact. Keeping in view the nature of the offence, evidence, complicity of the accused, submissions of the learned counsel for the parties, and without expressing any opinion on merits of the case, I am of the view that the applicant has made out a case for bail. Applicant is in jail since 18.10.2020. He has no criminal antecedents to his credit. There is no possibility of the applicant of fleeing away from the judicial process or tampering with the witnesses and, in case, the applicant is enlarged on bail, the applicant shall not misuse the liberty of bail. Let the applicant-Tilak Singh @ Debu be released on bail in the aforesaid case crime number on furnishing a personal bond and two sureties each in the like amount to the satisfaction of the court concerned with the following conditions which are being imposed in the interest of justice:- (i) THE APPLICANT SHALL FILE AN UNDERTAKING TO THE EFFECT THAT HE/SHE SHALL NOT SEEK ANY ADJOURNMENT ON THE DATE FIXED FOR EVIDENCE WHEN THE WITNESSES ARE PRESENT IN COURT. IN CASE OF DEFAULT OF THIS CONDITION, IT SHALL BE OPEN FOR THE TRIAL COURT TO TREAT IT AS ABUSE OF LIBERTY OF BAIL AND PASS ORDERS IN ACCORDANCE WITH LAW. (ii) THE APPLICANT SHALL REMAIN PRESENT BEFORE THE TRIAL COURT ON EACH DATE FIXED, EITHER PERSONALLY OR THROUGH HIS/HER COUNSEL. IN CASE OF HIS/HER ABSENCE, WITHOUT SUFFICIENT CAUSE, THE TRIAL COURT MAY PROCEED AGAINST HIM/HER UNDER SECTION 229-A IPC. (iii) IN CASE, THE APPLICANT MISUSES THE LIBERTY OF BAIL DURING TRIAL AND IN ORDER TO SECURE HIS/HER PRESENCE PROCLAMATION UNDER SECTION 82 CR.P.C., MAY BE ISSUED AND IF APPLICANT FAILS TO APPEAR BEFORE THE COURT ON THE DATE FIXED IN SUCH PROCLAMATION, THEN, THE TRIAL COURT SHALL INITIATE PROCEEDINGS AGAINST HIM/HER, IN ACCORDANCE WITH LAW, UNDER SECTION 174-A IPC. (iv) THE APPLICANT SHALL REMAIN PRESENT, IN PERSON, BEFORE THE TRIAL COURT ON DATES FIXED FOR (1) OPENING OF THE CASE, (2) FRAMING OF CHARGE AND (3) RECORDING OF STATEMENT UNDER SECTION 313 CR.P.C. IF IN THE OPINION OF THE TRIAL COURT ABSENCE OF THE APPLICANT IS DELIBERATE OR WITHOUT SUFFICIENT CAUSE, THEN IT SHALL BE OPEN FOR THE TRIAL COURT TO TREAT SUCH DEFAULT AS ABUSE OF LIBERTY OF BAIL AND PROCEED AGAINST THE HIM/HER IN ACCORDANCE WITH LAW. (v) THE TRIAL COURT MAY MAKE ALL POSSIBLE EFFORTS/ENDEAVOUR AND TRY TO CONCLUDE THE TRIAL WITHIN A PERIOD OF ONE YEAR AFTER THE RELEASE OF THE APPLICANT. However, it is made clear that any wilful violation of above conditions by the applicant, shall have serious repercussion on his/her bail so granted by this court and the trial court is at liberty to cancel the bail, after recording the reasons for doing so, in the given case of any of the condition mentioned above.

Correct Answer: These sections emphasize the severity of the charges and aim to protect minors from exploitation and abuse.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the applicant is facing serious charges under the IPC and POCSO Act, which are designed to protect minors against sexual offenses and other forms of exploitation. This highlights the judicial process's commitment to safeguarding the rights and welfare of minors involved in the case.

Passage: Madhya Pradesh High Court Bharti Kasde (Gond) vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh on 20 November, 2024. This petition u/S 482 r/W Sec 407 of Cr.P.C. is preferred against the order dated 04.11.2022 passed in SCATR No. 99/2021 by the learned First Additional Sessions Judge and Special Judge (POCSO) Act, Distt. Dewas. It is stated in the petition that P.S. Nemawar Distt. Dewas registered a missing person report and FIR bearing Crime No. 132/2021 for offence punishable u/S 363 of IPC with regard to missing of Mamta Bai, Divya, Rupali, Pawan and Pooja. After investigation, it was revealed that the accused in furtherance of a conspiracy killed Mamta Bai, Divya, Rupali, Pawan and Pooja on the intervening night of 13/14.05.2021. Accordingly, P.S. Nemawar filed final report u/S 173 of Cr.P.C. for offence punishable u/S 363, 302, 201, 120-B, 34 , 364, 376, 376(2)(n) of IPC; Section 3(2)(v) and 3(2)(va) of SC/ST(Prevention of Atrocities Act); Section 66B and 66C of the Information Technology Act and Section 5L and 6 of Prevention of Children from Sexual offences Act, 2012. The Department of Home, Govt. of M.P. vide Notification dated 29.12.2021 authorised further investigation of Crime No. 132/2021 to the Central Bureau of Investigation. The Govt. of India vide notification dated 11.04.2022 authorised the CBI to investigate into Crime No. 132/2021. The CBI(respondent no.2) filed supplementary final report (chargsheet) before the learned Special Judge. Learned (POCSO Act), Dewas took cognizance of the offence and declined to transfer the case to the Special Court (CBI), Indore via order dated 04.11.2022. Feeling aggrieved by non-transfer of the case to the Court of Special Judge(CBI), Indore, present petition is filed on the following grounds: 1. The learned trial Court at Dewas has no jurisdiction to try the case as the same was investigated by the CBI. 2. The Special Court(CBI), Indore alone has jurisdiction to try the matter. 3. The trial by a Court without jurisdiction would be in nullity.
Question: What is the rationale behind designating specific courts, like the Special Court under the POCSO Act, to handle cases involving child sexual offences and how does the petition filed in the case of Bharti Kasde (Gond) vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh reinforce this legal framework?
Correct Answer: Specialized courts ensure that sensitive cases, especially those involving children, are handled with the necessary expertise and child-friendly procedures that uphold victims' rights.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the POCSO Act establishes special courts to address the needs of child victims properly, and the petition emphasizes the requirement of appropriate jurisdiction to maintain the integrity of legal proceedings involving child sexual offences.

Passage: M.Sivakumar vs State Rep. By on 18 July, 2022. The Criminal Original Petition is filed under Section 482 of Cr.P.C., to call for the records and quash the F.I.R registered for the offence under Section 376, 417 and 506(ii) I.P.C against the petitioner in Crime No.6 of 2021 on the file of the Inspector of Police, W-10, All Women Police Station, Flower Bazaar, Chennai. The allegation in the F.I.R indicates that both the petitioner and the defacto complainant / 2nd respondent had love affair and thereafter the accused appeared to have refused to marry the defacto complainant, thereby F.I.R. came to be registered for the offence under Section 376, 417 and 506(ii) I.P.C. Now, the petitioner and the defacto complainant appeared before this Court and they were identified by the respective counsel and the investigation officer. Both of them submitted that they are married on 04.02.2022 and living happily together now. They have also filed a marriage registration certificate. Though the offence under Section 376 and 417 I.P.C are not compoundable, on a perusal of the F.I.R., it indicates that the parties had consensual relationship and no intention or deception could be notified to hold that there is no valid consent for intercourse. Further, both of them are married now and F.I.R came to be filed due to stained relationship before marriage. If the F.I.R taken into consideration for entirety, the same would not attract offence under Section 376 I.P.C as there is no ingredients to show that the consent was obtained by misconception of fact.
Question: Considering the situation described regarding M. Sivakumar who filed a petition to quash the FIR related to a consensual relationship that later involved accusations of sexual assault and is now married to the complainant, what legal principles should the court consider regarding the quashing of the FIR? Should the court prioritize the couple's current consensual relationship and marital status over the serious nature of the charges brought against Sivakumar? What potential claims for legal remedy should be analyzed in this context?
Correct Answer: The court should analyze the nature of the charges and consult on implications of public interest and consent.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that while the couple is now married, the nature of the allegations and their potential impact on public interest and justice must be considered. The law classifies the offenses under IPC as serious, and thus the court needs to analyze the consent and the dynamics of their relationship before making a decision.

Passage: Sri Ananda Poojari vs State By Chamarajpet Police on 12 July, 2018. This criminal appeal is filed under Section 374(2) of Cr.P.C praying to set aside the judgment/order of conviction and sentence dated:31.05.2016 passed by the LIII Addl. City Civil and Sessions Judge at Bengaluru City in Spl. Case No.188/2015 - Convicting the appellant/accused for the offence punishable under Section 9(L)(M)R/w Sec.10 of POCSO Act, 2012. The appellant/accused is sentenced to undergo R.I. for 5 years and pay fine of Rs.50,000/- in default of pay fine, he shall undergo R.I. for one fourth of the period prescribed for the substantive sentence. This appeal is against the judgment which convicted the accused under Section 9(L)(M) r/w Section 10 of Protection of Children from Sexual Offence Act, 2012 and sentencing him to five years of imprisonment. The victim in this case, Disha, was alone at home when the accused entered with a pretext and committed the sexual abuse. The victim screamed and the parents then filed a complaint leading to the arrest of the accused. The trial court held that the prosecution established the guilt against the accused based on the victim's testimony and the complaint made. However, the appeal argues against the conviction alleging that the evidence was not cogent or consistent. The Court found that while the trial had deficiencies concerning evidentiary support and reliance on family testimonies, the accused, having suffered judicial custody for over two years, had his sentence reduced, though the conviction under POCSO was modified due to inconsistencies in evidence conveyed by witnesses.
Question: Reflecting on the case of Sri Ananda Poojari, what does the appellate court decision reveal about the weight of testimonial evidence from the family of a victim in the context of POCSO Act violations? Also, analyze how the findings of inconsistencies in such testimonies informed the final verdict regarding the sentence reduction for the accused.
Correct Answer: The importance of testimonial evidence was reiterated, but inconsistencies diminished its overall impact leading to a sentence reduction.
Reasoning: The appellate court acknowledged the value of testimony from the family while simultaneously recognizing that inconsistencies raised doubts about the prosecution's narrative, thereby influencing the decision to reduce the sentence.

Passage: The petitioner apprehends arrest in Crime No.904/2017 of Pavaratty Police Station, Thrissur District. This is a typical case where the warring parties to a matrimonial dispute, without thinking about the consequences, make false and unfounded allegations resulting in irreparable damage to the reputation even of their child. The petitioner is working as a Village Officer in Elavally Village Office. The petitioner and his wife are married for the last 16 years and two girl children were born out of their wedlock. The father in law of the petitioner, after his retirement from service, unnecessarily started intruding into the affairs of the petitioner and his family leading to difference of opinion between the petitioner and his father-in-law. Unfortunately, despite spending 16 years with his wife together, the wife of the petitioner supported her father and stood against the petitioner leading to strain their matrimonial relationship. The members of the petitioner's family as well as the members of the wife of the petitioner's family met in the house of the petitioner on 18.07.2018 to sort out the differences between the petitioner, his wife and father-in-law. Unfortunately they could not able to succeed in solving their problems. Thereafter, on 27.07.2017, the wife of the petitioner has filed a complaint before the Pavaratty Police alleging that the petitioner had sexually abused their 4 year old daughter. Based on the above complaint, the police registered the above crime against the petitioner alleging offences punishable under Sections 5(m), 5(n), 6, 7, 8 r/w.9(1) of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012. The petitioner is being victimised by his wife to settle matter for which the daughter of the petitioner is unfortunately dragged into this by the wife of the petitioner. She did not realise the consequences of the petitioner being put behind bars on account of this false allegation. The learned counsel for the petitioner has submitted that the allegation levelled against him are false and no such incident was happened. It was also argued that there was an altercation between the petitioner and his wife and they were living separately due to difference of opinion and there was a settlement talk among the petitioner, his wife and father-in-law. But, it became futile. Therefore, in order to pressurize the petitioner to settle the matter, the 2nd female child was brought into this by stating that she was sexually assaulted by the petitioner. In the meanwhile, the matter was mediated by the Kerala High Court Mediation Centre and it was settled. It was also decided to quash the entire proceedings which was taken in this crime. In fact, even though the crime was registered on account of the strained relationship between the petitioner and the 2nd respondent, now the entire matter has been settled. Considering all these facts, I find that the crime was registered on account of the strained relationship between the petitioner and the 2nd respondent. Therefore, I find that it is unjust to keep the petitioner, who is none other the father of the victim behind the bars. Moreover, the matrimonial dispute between the petitioner and the 2nd respondent have also been settled. Under these circumstances, I am inclined to grant anticipatory bail to the petitioner on the following conditions: The petitioner shall surrender before the Special Court for POCSO cases, Thrissur on or before 07.12.2018 between 10.00.a.m. and 11.00.a.m. and after interrogation, he shall be released on bail on executing a bond for Rs.50,000/- (Rupees Fifty thousand only) with two solvent sureties each for the like sum to the satisfaction of the jurisdictional Judge. The petitioner shall appear before the Investigating Officer as and when requested.
Question: In the context of child protection laws as illustrated in the passage, what complexities arise when a legal dispute stems from familial disputes, especially regarding allegations of sexual abuse, and how can this affect the child involved?
Correct Answer: Child protection laws may inadvertently be misused in personal conflicts, complicating the legitimate pursuit of justice when false allegations are made.
Reasoning: The passage depicts a scenario where a dispute leads to false allegations of child abuse, highlighting the potential misuse of child protection laws in familial conflicts. This scenario complicates the judicial process, affecting both the accused and the child, who is drawn into the conflict.

Passage: The State Of Karnataka vs Sri Brahmadeva ruling from the Karnataka High Court involved significant legal determinations regarding child protection laws and the evidentiary requirements for prosecuting sexual offenses against minors. The victim in this case reported her traumatic experiences of abuse over two years while under the care of a Child Care Institution but faced an acquittal of the accused due to a lack of corroborative evidence and witness challenges. This raised crucial questions about how child testimonies are treated under the law and the expectations for corroboration or additional substantiation of claims made by young victims. The trial court effectively highlighted the critical balance needed between protecting the rights of the accused and the rights of the child victim—a balance that remains a contentious issue within the framework of child protection laws.
Question: What legal protections can be enacted or reinforced to ensure that child victims in cases like State Of Karnataka vs Sri Brahmadeva are treated fairly in court, particularly regarding the need for corroboration of testimony?
Correct Answer: Implement specialized legal training for law enforcement and judicial officials to handle children's testimony appropriately.
Reasoning: The ruling indicates the challenges faced by child witnesses; thus, providing training will ensure that their testimonies are taken seriously and treated with the sensitivity required, potentially reducing the burden of corroboration in initial proceedings.

Passage: State Of Karnataka vs Kunduwada Basavaraj on 18 July, 2024 - IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU DATED THIS THE 18TH DAY OF JULY, 2024. This criminal appeal is filed under Section 378(1) & (3) of Cr.P.C. praying to grant leave to appeal and set aside the judgment and order of acquittal dated 30.06.2016 passed by the Principal District and Sessions Judge, Davanagere in S.C.NO.1/2014 - acquitting the respondent for the offence punishable under Sections 304B read with Section 34 of IPC and Sec.3 and 4 of D.P.Act. The marriage of accused No.3 and deceased Kavitha was solemnized on 12.11.2010 in Davanagere. At the time of marriage, on the demand of the accused, the parents of Kavitha had given cash of Rs.3,00,000/-, 20 tholas of gold jewelry, one motorcycle and marriage expenses as dowry. The accused lived cordially with the deceased till she gave birth to a female child who was born with physical and mental disability. The accused subjected Kavitha to physical and mental cruelty demanding additional dowry and abusing that she has given birth to a physically and mentally challenged child. The accused were harassing her saying that she should kill the said child or abandon the same somewhere and she should bring money for the treatment of the child. Being unable to withstand such cruelty, on 10.07.2013 between 05.00 p.m and 08.00 p.m. Kavitha committed suicide by hanging herself in the house of the accused. The narrative illustrates an unsettling reality of dowry-related cruelty and the importance of judicial intervention. A reassessment of the judicial attitudes towards domestic violence is necessary to ensure enhanced legal protections.
Question: Considering the disturbing narrative of Kavitha's suicide, what measures should be instituted to reassess judicial attitudes towards cases of domestic violence arising from dowry that ensure victims receive essential legal protections? How does this relate to the broader societal need for change?
Correct Answer: All of the above are necessary mechanisms.
Reasoning: To truly address the deep-seated issues surrounding dowry-related domestic violence, an overhaul of judicial attitudes towards these cases is essential, ensuring that all victims of such violence receive the protections they deserve, as indicated in Kavitha’s heartbreaking story.

Passage: In Mohit Rajput vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh, the legal discussions centered on the conflicting perceptions of consent when involving minors in sexual relationships. The petitioner claimed that the victim consented to their relationship, thereby requesting bail. However, the prosecution presented the argument that, under the POCSO Act, any sexual relations involving minors are inherently non-consensual, serving to protect minors from exploitation and recognizing the incapacity to consent due to their age. The court's dismissal of the bail application closely examined the implications of consent and the stringent requirements of the POCSO Act.
Question: What does the court's decision in the Mohit Rajput case reveal about the judicial approach to cases involving minors and consent, particularly in relation to the definitions set forth in the POCSO Act?
Correct Answer: The decision indicates that the judiciary firmly upholds that no minor can legally consent to sexual relations, supporting the POCSO Act's aim to protect vulnerable individuals.
Reasoning: The passage illustrates that the court's ruling emphasized the POCSO Act's protective nature, firmly establishing that minors are incapable of giving legal consent, thus reinforcing statutory safeguards against exploitation.

Passage: This Criminal Original Petition has been filed to call for the records pending on the file of the Fast Track Mahila Court, Namakkal pertaining to Spl.C.C.No.2 of 2022 and to quash the same. The learned counsel for the petitioner submits that Ms. Sumathy, a Women Rural Welfare Officer, filed the complaint based on the child marriage of the victim girl who is a minor. The legal implications of marrying a minor are severe as set forth by the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act. Even if both parties agree to the marriage, it is still a punishable act under law. The victim has now reached maturity, and the couple has since legally married. This invocation of Section 482 illustrates the court's ability to prevent an abuse of its processes when the situation permits a resolution.
Question: What potential remedies does the High Court have in child marriage cases where the victim has matured and the parties have resolved their issues amicably? Are there specific legal criteria that must be met for quashing such cases?
Correct Answer: The High Court may quash proceedings if the victim affirms their consent to the marriage, and public interest is not negatively impacted.
Reasoning: According to the passage, the High Court can exercise its powers to quash criminal proceedings when it serves the ends of justice. The circumstances of the case demonstrate that the victim, now a mature individual, has consented to the marriage, suggesting a lack of public interest in further prosecution.

Present: SMT.VIJAYALAXMI S.UPANAL, M.A., LL.M., LIV ADDL., CITY CIVIL & SESSIONS JUDGE, BANGALORE CITY. Spl.C.C.No.463/2014 COMPLAINANT State by Hebbal Police, Bangalore City. (By Learned Public Prosecutor) -Vs - ACCUSED Puneeth, S/o.Late Umesh, 22yrs, R/at.3rd Cross, B-Narayanapura, Mahadevapura, Bangalore-48. (By Sri.T.D-Advocate.) 1. Opinion of the Judge Accused found not guilty. 9. Order of sentence As per the final order. JUDGMENT The Police Inspector, Hebbal Police Station, Bangalore has submitted charge sheet against the accused for the offences punishable under Section 363, 366, 376 of IPC and Section 5(l) read with Section 6 of POCSO Act, 2012 in Crime No.177/2014. The brief facts of the prosecution case are as under: The accused was in love with Cw.2-the minor victim girl, aged about 17 years. On 15-07-2014 the accused by inducing the victim minor girl kidnapped her from the custody of her lawful guardian, with an intention to compel her to marry him and raped her forcibly and repeatedly. This case illustrates the complexities of underage relationships and whether the young ages of both parties could affect the legal repercussions involved. It is essential for the judicial system to balance protection for minors while also maintaining the integrity of legal proceedings in cases involving accusations. The case highlights the need for clear guidelines on how to navigate such intimate matters while respecting both the rights of victims and defendants under the law.
Question: In light of the passage, what should be the primary consideration for the legal system when addressing cases involving minors as both victims and voluntary participants in intimate relationships, particularly in terms of their rights and how to ensure just outcomes?
Correct Answer: The legal system should prioritize the rights and welfare of minors, ensuring legal proceedings take into account their unique circumstances while providing protection from abuse and exploitation.
Reasoning: The passage emphasizes the complexities involved in cases where minors are both victims and active participants. The key is to ensure that minors’ rights are upheld, affording them protection against exploitation while balancing the judicial process to remain fair to all parties involved.

Passage: Sc No. 58010/16 State vs . Vijay & Ors on 25 July, 2017 located in the Court of MS. Seema Maini. The case involves three accused persons who are charged with following, stalking, and sexually harassing a 14-year-old victim, K. The victims testified about being misbehaved with while returning from school, where they were caught and threatened by the accused. The victim initially told her Bua about the incident but nobody took any action until her maternal uncle brought her to the police. The accused were arrested, but due to lack of witnesses or corroborative evidence, they were acquitted. The victim did not suffer any actual injury, hence not entitled to compensation.
Question: What legal actions could be taken for the victim K in regards to her case against the accused based on the judgment outcome, and what implications does the lack of physical evidence or injury have on her potential compensation as discussed in the judgment?
Correct Answer: K could file for civil compensation despite the acquittal since she experienced harassment.
Reasoning: Even though the accused were acquitted in criminal court due to lack of corroborative evidence, civil action for harassment and emotional distress could still be pursued, as these are separate legal matters.

Passage:
Sri Javed Shafiq Rao vs State Of Karnataka on 10 February, 2025

The case explores the implications of responsibility in negligence claims regarding child safety in residential complexes. In this instance, the petitioner, as Vice President of a property management firm, contested allegations implying negligence led to a child’s electrocution in a swimming pool area. The complaints center on the failure to maintain safety standards and the ensuing tragic death. However, as the management firm had already handed over the facility to a governing homeowners association, arguments were presented that liability for safety and operational control lay with that entity. Thus, the Court assessed the evidence and emphasized that if the petitioner had indeed divested control, imposing liability on him could result in an abuse of legal process. The conclusion emphasized the importance of establishing the chain of responsibility in child safety incidents in residential settings.
Question: In light of the case between Sri Javed Shafiq Rao and the State of Karnataka, what are the implications when an entity like Prestige Property Management hands over control of facilities responsible for child safety to another body, and what should victims' families understand about their rights and legal recourse in such scenarios?
Correct Answer: The transfer of control limits liability for the new management resulting in lesser recourse for victims' families.
Reasoning: The passage emphasizes that if responsibility for a child’s safety has been transferred to a homeowners association, the original management may not be held liable for incidents arising under the new management's control, thereby informing victims' families about the challenges they might face in seeking justice.

Passage: On the material placed before us it is beyond the pale of any doubt or controversy that the victim of the offences statedly committed by the appellant was a 'child' within the meaning of the expression defined in Section 2(1)(d) of POCSO Act, she being below the age of 18 years at the relevant point of time. Given the nature of offences involved, she is entitled to the protection envisaged by law in Section 33(7) of POCSO Act. To put it simply, the special court was duty bound to ensure that her identity was 'not disclosed at any time'. As we shall note later, this precaution was given a go-by during the proceedings before the trial judge more than once.
Question: What legal protections are afforded to child victims under the POCSO Act as highlighted in the judgment, and what steps must the courts take to uphold these protections during the trial process?
Correct Answer: The court must keep the identity of the child victim confidential and ensure that disclosures are only made when absolutely necessary and justified.
Reasoning: The passage clearly articulates the statutory obligation of the special court under the POCSO Act to protect the identity of child victims, emphasizing that any disclosure should only follow a thorough judicial examination considering the best interest of the child.

Passage: The Rajappa vs Dakshinamurthy case involved critical analysis concerning child marriage and offenses against minors under Indian law. The High Court's ruling reinforced essential principles of justice whereby accusations against parents need to be directly actionable with clear evidence. The court quashed the charges for the accused parents and elaborated on the abuse of process doctrine in legal proceedings involving vulnerable populations such as children, thereby emphasizing the legal system's role in protecting the innocent.
Question: In the context of the Rajappa vs Dakshinamurthy case, how did the court's ruling contribute to the legal protection of vulnerable individuals against wrongful accusations under child marriage laws?
Correct Answer: The court reinforced protections whereby charges against parents require concrete evidence to justify legal action, thus safeguarding the innocent.
Reasoning: The decision established a crucial boundary in legal terms, ensuring that parents can only be implicated based on clear evidence and preventing wrongful prosecutions.

Passage: Abdulla Ainulhaq Ansari vs The State Of Maharashtra And Anr on 16 December, 2021. This appeal emphasizes how the appellate court critically analyses the judgments made in lower courts regarding child sexual abuse cases. Primarily, it examines whether the evidence provided by the prosecution was sufficient to support the conviction under the POCSO Act. Experienced judges reviewed if the legal requirements were satisfied during trials, particularly methods of gathering testimonies and evidence from the victim. Reflecting on the case, it signifies the importance of judicial oversight in retaining justice in cases involving child abuse and establishing the necessity for continual evaluation of how laws are practiced in the legal system to ensure children's safety and perpetrator accountability.
Question: How does the appellate court's review process contribute to ensuring justice in child sexual abuse cases under the POCSO Act, specifically in terms of evidence evaluation and judicial oversight, as mentioned in the passage?
Correct Answer: The appellate court's review highlights and evaluates evidence rigorously, ensuring that the processes align with legal standards aimed at protecting child victims under the POCSO Act.
Reasoning: The passage underlines the role of the appellate court in scrutinizing lower court judgments and emphasizing the critical need for robust evidence gathering and evaluation to uphold justice for child victims, thereby reinforcing judicial accountability.

Passage:
Akhtar Khan vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh on 14 July, 2023

Madhya Pradesh High Court
Akhtar Khan vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh on 14 July, 2023
Author: Rohit Arya
Bench: Rohit Arya
1
IN THE HIGH COURT OF MADHYA PRADESH AT GWALIOR CRA No. 1152 of 2023 (AKHTAR KHAN Vs THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH AND OTHERS)
Dated : 14-07-2023 Shri F.A. Shah- Advocate for the appellant. Dr. Anjali Gyanani - Public Prosecutor for respondent/State. Heard on I.A. No.7003 of 2023, which is first application under Section 389(1) Cr.P.C. filed on behalf of appellant-Akhtar Khan seeking suspension of sentence and grant of bail. Appellant-Akhtar Khan stood convicted under Section 4(2) of POCSO Act and sentenced to undergo 20 years' R.I. with a fine of Rs.2,000/- and under Section 9 of Prohibition of Child Marriage Act and sentenced to undergo 2 years' R.I with a fine of Rs.1,000/- with default stipulations vide judgment of conviction and order of sentence dated 15/12/2022 passed by Special Judge (POCSO Act), Shivpuri (M.P.) in Special Case No.124/2019. Present appellant so far has undergone incarceration of about 17 months in toto, as transpired during the course of hearing. Co-accused/Appellant No.1 in Criminal Appeal No.838 of 2023-Shakil Khan is the biological father of the prosecutrix as after the demise of his first wife and mother of the prosecutrix, he married to co-accused Shehnaz Bano. As per prosecution story, the prosecutrix claiming herself to be 14 years of age, on 20/09/2019, lodged an FIR to the effect that for the last 4-5 years since the time her mother expired, she was ill-treated by her step-mother Shehnaz Bano off and on. She, therefore, had left the house and started living with her grand-parents. Thereafter, on 1/1/2019, while she was staying with her maternal uncle's house, step-mother Shehnaz came there and against her wishes brought her back. Thereafter, she started searching for the match though she was not major. She manipulated her marriage (Nikah) with accused/appellant Akhtar and Farukh Khan had reduced in writing the process of Nikah. After so-called solemnization of marriage (Nikah), she was subjected to forcible sexual abuse against her wishes by Akhtar from 24/08/2019 to 28/08/2019. Thereafter, accused/appellant Akhtar called her grand-parents to the Kota Court and whereafter, signing of certain documents, she was given in custody of her grand-parents. Thereafter, she came back to her grand-parents' house from Kota. It is alleged that appellant Akhtar had also threatened the prosecutrix with dire consequences, if she divulged the factum of marriage and that of sexual abuse. In the aforesaid backdrop, the prosecutrix filed the complaint and the same was registered at Crime No.213/19. Based thereupon, investigation was set in motion. The Investigating Officer, upon collection of incriminating evidence, filed challan. The Special Court upon critical evaluation of the evidence placed on record convicted and sentenced the present appellant as aforesaid. Learned counsel for the appellant while taking exception to the impugned judgement submits that the prosecutrix in her statement before the Court has clearly stated that her date of birth is 11/10/2000 as recorded in Aadhar Card and therefore, she was major as on the date of incident. However, the trial Court in Para-20 of the judgement, ignored the same and relied upon her date of birth as per the certificate of the Municipal Corporation, Kota (Ex.P/10) as 11/10/2005. That apart, she has turned hostile and belied the story of prosecution. Shakil Khan, co-convict/appellant No.1 in Criminal Appeal No.838 of 2023, has already been admitted to suspension of sentence and grant of bail vide order dated 27.04.2023. On such grounds, learned counsel prays for suspension of sentence and grant of bail to the present appellant. Per contra, learned Public Prosecutor for respondent/State, while opposing the application supporting the judgment impugned submits that looking to the gravity of offence, no exception can be taken in the matter of suspension of sentence and grant of bail to the present appellant. Upon hearing learned counsel for the parties, though this Court refrains from commenting upon rival contentions touching merits of the case but regard being had to the fact that co-convict/appellant No.1 -Shakil Khan in Criminal Appeal No.838 of 2023 has already been admitted to suspension of sentence and grant of bail vide order dated 27.04.2023, the present appellant has so far undergone incarceration of 17 months in toto coupled with the fact that appeal is of the year 2023 and there is no likelihood of its early hearing in near future and therefore, in the obtaining facts and circumstances, we hereby extend the benefit of suspension of sentence and grant of bail to present appellant-Akhtar Khan. Accordingly, I.A.7003 of 2023 stands allowed and it is directed that the jail sentence of appellant-Akhtar Khan shall remain suspended during pendency of present appeal and he shall be released on bail, subject to verification of amount of fine being deposited and on his furnishing a personal bond in the sum of Rs.2,00,000/- (Rupees Two Lacs only) with one solvent surety in the like amount to the satisfaction of the Trial Court. Appellant-Akhtar Khan is directed to appear before the Registry of this Court first on 13.09.2023 and on other subsequent dates as may be fixed by Office in this behalf. Observations on facts, if any, are only for the purpose of disposal of the application for suspension of sentence and shall have no bearing on the merits of the appeal. Certified copy as per rules. (ROHIT ARYA) (SANJEEV S KALGAONKAR) JUDGE JUDGE pd
Question: Considering the conviction of Akhtar Khan under the POCSO Act and the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act, what are the implications of the court's decision to suspend his sentence and grant him bail for future prosecution in cases similar to this involving minors in relation to their rights and protection?
Correct Answer: It sets a precedent that can undermine the severity of laws protecting minors if abusers can frequently be granted bail.
Reasoning: The suspension of Akhtar Khan's sentence and granting of bail, despite serious charges, can potentially undermine the credibility of child protection laws and may set a troubling precedent for other similar cases, thus affecting the future judicial response to protecting minor victims.
