Passage: Shadab vs State Of U.P. And Another on 6 January, 2021. HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT ALLAHABAD. Case :- CRIMINAL MISC. BAIL APPLICATION No. - 47508 of 2020. Applicant :- Shadab. Opposite Party :- State of U.P. and Another. Counsel for Applicant :- Praveen Kumar Srivastava. Counsel for Opposite Party :- G.A. Hon'ble Samit Gopal,J. Heard Sri Praveen Kumar Srivastava, learned counsel for the applicant, Sri Brij Bhushan Upadhyay, learned AGA for the State and perused the material on record. This bail application under Section 439 of Code of Criminal Procedure has been filed by the applicant Shadab, seeking enlargement on bail during trial in connection with Case Crime No. 343 of 2020, under Sections 363, 366, 376 I.P.C. and 3/4 POCSO Act, registered at P.S. Shahaswan, District Budaun. Learned counsel for the applicant argued that the victim as per certificate of Chief Medical Officer, Budaun, copy of which is annexed as annexure no. 5(at page-27) to the affidavit, was opined to be about 17 years of age. It is argued that the statement of the prosecutrix was recorded under Section 164 Cr.P.C., copy of which is annexed as annexure no. 6 to the affidavit, in which she stated that she went with the applicant along with her sister and her child who is divorced, and the boyfriend of her elder sister out of her own sweet will to Delhi and remained there for about 18 days in a room. Further she stated that the applicant did not commit any illegal act with her and all the four persons lived in one room together. It is argued that the applicant has been falsely implicated in the present case as no offence against him is made out. Learned counsel for the applicant argued that the applicant has no other criminal antecedents as stated in para-16 of the affidavit and is in jail since 24.8.2020. Per contra, learned AGA has opposed the prayer for bail but could not dispute the arguments raised above. After perusing the record in the light of the submissions made at the bar and after taking an overall view of all the facts and circumstances of this case, the nature of evidence and also the absence of any convincing material to indicate the possibility of tampering with the evidence, this Court is of the view that the applicant may be enlarged on bail. Let the applicant- Shadab, be released on bail in the aforesaid case crime number on furnishing a personal bond and two sureties each in the like amount to the satisfaction of the court concerned with the following conditions which are being imposed in the interest of justice: i) The applicant will not tamper with prosecution evidence and will not harm or harass the victim/complainant in any manner whatsoever. ii) The applicant will abide the orders of court, will attend the court on every date and will not delay the disposal of trial in any manner whatsoever. (iii) The applicant shall file an undertaking to the effect that he shall not seek any adjournment on the date fixed for evidence when the witnesses are present in court. In case of default of this condition, it shall be open for the trial court to treat it as abuse of liberty of bail and pass orders in accordance with law. (iv) The applicant will not misuse the liberty of bail in any manner whatsoever. In case the applicant misuses the liberty of bail during trial and in order to secure his presence proclamation under section 82 Cr.P.C., may be issued and if applicant fails to appear before the court on the date fixed in such proclamation, then, the trial court shall initiate proceedings against him, in accordance with law, under section 174-A I.P.C. (V) The applicant shall remain present, in person, before the trial court on dates fixed for (1) opening of the case, (2) framing of charge and (3) recording of statement under Section 313 Cr.P.C. If in the opinion of the trial court absence of the applicant is deliberate or without sufficient cause, then it shall be open for the trial court to treat such default as abuse of liberty of bail and proceed against him in accordance with law and the trial court may proceed against him under Section 229-A IPC. (vi) The trial court may make all possible efforts/endeavour and try to conclude the trial expeditiously after the release of the applicant. The identity, status and residential proof of sureties will be verified by court concerned and in case of breach of any of the conditions mentioned above, court concerned will be at liberty to cancel the bail and send the applicant to prison. The bail application is allowed. The party shall file computer generated copy of such order downloaded from the official website of High Court Allahabad. The computer generated copy of such order shall be self-attested by the counsel of the party concerned. The concerned Court/Authority/Official shall verify the authenticity of such computerized copy of the order from the official website of High Court Allahabad and shall make a declaration of such verification in writing. (Samit Gopal,J.) Order Date :- 6.1.2021.
Question: What criteria did the court follow in deciding to grant bail to Shadab while ensuring measures were implemented to protect the victim, and how do these criteria reflect the balance of fairness in the justice system?
Correct Answer: The court balanced the requirements of justice by assessing the evidence, the circumstances of the case, and setting conditions to protect the victim and maintain trial integrity.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the court carefully reviewed the evidence and circumstances before allowing bail while imposing essential conditions that protect the victim’s welfare and ensure the trial process remains fair and just.

Passage: Ajay Kumar Jatav vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh on 6 January, 2023. The applicant is in custody since 22.08.2022 in connection with Crime No.249/2021 for the offences punishable under Sections 363, 366, 376, 506 of IPC and Section 3/4 of POCSO Act. A s per prosecution story, the applicant had manipulated the prosecutrix and on false promise of marriage had called her to his house. The prosecutrix after coming back home had narrated the whole incident to her parents and FIR was lodged at Crime No.249/2021 under Sections 363, 366, 376, 506 of IPC and Section 3/4 of POCSO Act. The learned Panel Lawyer opposes the bail application with submissions that the prosecutrix was a minor, therefore, irrespective of her consent, the alleged act of sexual abuse is a serious crime against a child.
Question: How does the age of the victim influence the legal proceedings in the case of Ajay Kumar Jatav vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh, especially under child protection laws like the POCSO Act?
Correct Answer: The age of the victim is crucial as it determines the application of the POCSO Act, which prohibits any sexual activity with minors regardless of consent.
Reasoning: The passage clearly states that the prosecutrix is a minor, emphasizing that under the POCSO Act, a minor cannot consent to sexual activities. This age stipulation is significant in legal proceedings, impacting the nature of the charges and the available legal protections.

Passage: Kerala High Court, Manikandan vs State Of Kerala on 14 July, 2020. The petitioner is the sole accused in SC No.585 of 2019 pending before the First Additional Sessions Court (Special Court), Palakkad. The case against the petitioner originated on registration of Crime No.466 of 2018 at the Pudunagaram Police Station for offences under Section 376 (2) (n) of IPC and Sections 6 r/w 5 (j)(ii)(I) of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act. The allegation which led to the registration of the Crime is that the petitioner and the victim had eloped on 18.05.2018, when the victim was a minor and that, after marrying the victim, the petitioner had subjected her to penetrating sexual assault and had thereby committed the offences. The aunt of the victim, who had filed the complaint leading to registration of the crime has filed an affidavit stating that the victim, whose parents were no more, had eloped with the petitioner and had married him. A legal marriage was conducted on 08.12.2019, after the victim became major and the marriage was registered on 25.02.2020. One girl child is born out of the relationship between the petitioner and the victim and that the petitioner and the victim are living as husband and wife. The victim has also filed an affidavit reiterating the above facts and further stating that she has no subsisting grievance against the petitioner. It is stated that continuance of the harmonious marital life of the petitioner and the victim require quashing of the criminal proceedings. I heard the learned Public Prosecutor, who, on instructions, submits that the petitioner has no criminal antecedent and that the affidavit seeking quashing of the proceedings was submitted on her own volition. It is submitted that, though the sexual relationship of the petitioner with the victim was immediately prior to the victim becoming major, the fact that the petitioner had married the victim after she became a major and that both of them are leading a harmonious married life, can be taken into consideration. I take note of the fact that both the petitioner and the victim are rustic villagers and that after eloping from their parental homes, had married and lived as husband and wife. Immediately after the victim attaining 18 years, their marriage was registered and they are leading a harmonious marital life. Moreover, in view of the settlement arrived at between the parties, there is no possibility of the criminal proceedings ending in conviction. As such, continuance of the proceeding will amount to an abuse of process of court and hence, in view of the legal position set out by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Madan Mohan Abbot v. State of Punjab, Gian Singh v. State of Punjab and another, there is no impediment in granting the relief. In the aforementioned peculiar circumstances, I deem it appropriate to allow the Crl.M.C, even though the offences alleged are under Section 376 (2) (n) of IPC and Section 6 r/w 5 (j)(ii)(I) of the POCSO Act. In the result, Crl.M.C is allowed; the proceedings in SC No.585 of 2019 pending before the First Additional Sessions Court (Special Court), Palakkad, is quashed.
Question: In the context of the case of Manikandan vs State of Kerala, how does the legal interpretation of the victim’s marriage post-majority influence the court's decision on quashing the sexual assault charges? What key factors did the court consider in relation to the victim's change in status?
Correct Answer: The court viewed the marriage as a key factor demonstrating the consensual nature of the relationship after the victim attained adulthood.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the court acknowledged the marriage after the victim turned 18 as a significant factor, influencing their decision to quash the criminal charges, highlighting the transformation of the victim's status and the implications for consent.

Passage: Deepu B @ Kuttan vs State Of Kerala Represented By Public Prosecutor on 21 August, 2020. This Bail Application filed under Section 439 of Criminal Procedure Code was heard through Video Conference. The Petitioner is the accused in Crime No.263 of 2020 of Hosdurg Police Station, Kasargod District. The above case is registered against the petitioner alleging offences punishable under Section 376AB of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) r/w. Sections 6 r/w 5(m)(n) of the Protection of Children From Sexual Offences Act (POCSO Act). The petitioner is in custody from 20.6.2020 onwards. The prosecution case is that, on 1.4.2020, the accused sexually abused a minor girl aged 4 ½ years by touching the genital organ of the victim. It is the case of the defacto complainant, the mother of the victim girl that, the child complained about a pain on her private parts and when the defacto complainant enquired about the same, the child disclosed to the defacto complainant that the petitioner committed the above activities. The alleged incident happened on 1.4.2020. The F.I. statement was given on 12.5.2020. The petitioner was arrested on 20.6.2020. Heard the learned counsel for the petitioner and the learned public prosecutor. The learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that, the petitioner is innocent in this case. The counsel submitted that, this is a false case foisted against the petitioner by the mother of the victim. The counsel submitted that, there is some difference of opinion between the defacto complainant and her husband. The petitioner is a close relative of the husband of the defacto complainant. The counsel submitted that, the petitioner supported the husband of the defacto complainant when there was a dispute between the defacto complainant and her husband. According to the petitioner, at that time, the defacto complainant threatened that, she will foist a false case against the petitioner. The counsel submitted that, the husband of the defacto complainant filed an affidavit before this Court. The counsel submitted that, in the affidavit, it is stated by the father of the victim, who is the husband of the defacto complainant that, this is a false case foisted against the petitioner. The father of the victim clearly stated that the petitioner is innocent in this case. The learned public prosecutor seriously opposed this bail application. The public prosecutor submitted that the allegations against the petitioner are very serious. The allegation is that, the petitioner committed sexual assault on a minor girl aged 4 ½ years. The public prosecutor submitted that the petitioner is not entitled orders under Section 439 Cr.P.C. at this stage. After hearing both sides, I think, this bail application can be allowed, on stringent conditions. Admittedly, the petitioner is in custody from 20.6.2020 onwards. The alleged incident happened on 1.4.2020 and the F.I.statement was given only on 12.5.2020. Admittedly, there is some matrimonial dispute between the defact complainant and her husband. It is also an admitted fact that, the petitioner is a close relative of the husband of the defact complainant. It is true that, an affidavit is filed by the father of the victim girl exonerating the petitioner from the allegation. I do not want to make any observation about the contents in the affidavit at this stage. This is a matter to be investigated by the investigating officer. Considering the entire facts and circumstances of this case, I think, this bail application can be allowed on stringent conditions. There can be a direction to the petitioner not to enter the jurisdictional limit of Hosdurg Police Station till a final report in Crime No.263 of 2020 of Hosdurg Police Station, is filed. Moreover, considering the need to follow social distancing norms inside prisons so as to avert the spread of the novel Corona Virus Pandemic, the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Re: Contagion of COVID-19 Virus In Prisons case (Suo Motu Writ Petition(C) No.1 of 2020) and a Full Bench of this Court in W.P(C)No.9400 of 2020 issued various salutary directions for minimizing the number of inmates inside prisons. Moreover, it is a well accepted principle that, the bail is the rule and the jail is the exception. The Hon'ble Supreme Court in Chidambaram P. v. Directorate of Enforcement observed that, the basic jurisprudence relating to bail remains the same inasmuch as the grant of bail is the rule and refusal is the exception so as to ensure that the accused has the opportunity of securing fair trial. Considering the dictum laid down in the above decision and considering the facts and circumstances of this case, this Bail Application is allowed with the following directions: 1. The petitioner shall be released on bail on executing a bond for Rs.50,000/- (Rupees Fifty Thousand only) with two solvent sureties each for the like sum to the satisfaction of the jurisdictional Court; 2. The petitioner shall appear before the Investigating Officer for interrogation as and when required. The petitioner shall co-operate with the investigation and shall not, directly or indirectly make any inducement, threat or promise to any person acquainted with the facts of the case so as to dissuade him from disclosing such facts to the Court or to any police officer; 3. The petitioner shall not leave India without permission of the jurisdictional Court; 4. The petitioner shall not commit an offence similar to the offence of which he is accused, or suspected, of the commission of which he is suspected; 5. The petitioner shall not enter the jurisdictional limit of Hosdurg Police Station, except for the purposes mentioned above, till a final report in Crime No.263 of 2020 of Hosdurge Police Station is filed. 6. The petitioner shall strictly abide by the various guidelines issued by the State Government and Central Government with respect to keeping of social distancing in the wake of Covid 19 pandemic; 7. If any of the above conditions are violated by the petitioner, the jurisdictional Court can cancel the bail in accordance to law, even though the bail is granted by this Court. Sd/- P.V.KUNHIKRISHNAN JUDGE

Passage: Juvenile Delinquent 'S' vs State Of U.P. Thru. Prin. Secy. Home Lko. ... on 25 March, 2025. The present criminal revision under Section 102 of Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015 has been filed against the order dated 23.09.2024 passed by the learned Juvenile Justice Board, Pratapgarh wherein bail application of the juvenile revisionist has been rejected. A first information report in Case Crime No.293 of 2023 was lodged under Sections 363, 366, 376 IPC and 3/4 POCSO Act. The prosecutrix had gone outside for toilet and did not come back after sufficient long length of time. It was found that the prosecutrix has been lured by the revisionist and was beaten and insulted by the family of the revisionist and her garments were torn and she informed about the sexual assault... Considering the fact that the juvenile has been in jail for more than one year and also that he has married the prosecutrix and they have a child, the revision is allowed and the orders dated 23.09.2024 and 18.12.2024 are set aside. The juvenile is released on bail under certain conditions.
Question: How might the outcome of this case be seen as setting a precedent for future juvenile cases in relation to bail and release based on familial status, given the court's decision to grant bail to juvenile delinquent 'S' despite serious allegations?

Reasoning: The decision acknowledges the juvenile's responsibilities as a husband and father, suggesting that such familial connections could play a significant role in judicial decisions regarding bail in future juvenile cases.

Passage: Kuldeep vs State Of Haryana on 25 April, 2018. The petitioner seeks bail pending trial in FIR No.156 dated 18.03.2017, under Section 342 IPC and Section 8 of the POCSO Act. It is contended that the petitioner has been falsely implicated due to family quarrels and an accident he suffered. The medical evidence does not substantiate the allegations against him, as no injury was detected on the victim. The complainant has also admitted to prior disputes. The State has opposed the bail, but there is no evidence that the petitioner will abscond or influence witnesses. The court allowed the petition for bail, ordering that the petitioner not contact the victim or witnesses, clarifying that the decision does not reflect on the merits of the case.
Question: How can the legal principles established in the ruling of Kuldeep vs State Of Haryana demonstrate the balance between ensuring the accused's right to bail and protecting the child victim's interests under the POCSO Act?
Correct Answer: Legal principles necessitate rigorous evaluation of all involved parties' rights while ensuring protective measures for child victims.
Reasoning: The ruling showcases the court's approach in reconciling the rights of the accused with the imperative to protect the child victim's welfare, establishing a dual focus that upholds justice and safety, in accordance with legal standards articulated by the POCSO Act.

Passage: The above appeals arise from S.C. No.135 of 2014 on the files of the Additional District and Sessions Court, Ernakulam. There were five accused in the case. Accused 1, 2 and 5 among them, are the appellants in the appeals. Accused 3 and 4 in the case were though convicted and sentenced under Section 354 IPC, they have not preferred any appeal challenging their conviction and sentence in the case. Among the appellants, the first accused stands convicted and sentenced under Section 376(1) of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), the second accused stands convicted and sentenced under Section 109 read with Sections 354 and 376(1) IPC as also under Section 23 of the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2000 and the fifth accused stands convicted and sentenced under Sections 354 and 376(1) IPC. The second accused is the mother of the victim. The father of the victim is no more. The first accused was a regular visitor in the house of the second accused. The fifth accused is a person running a shop in the close proximity of the house of the victim. The case which culminated in the conviction of the accused was one registered at the instance of the Child Welfare Committee, Ernakulam. In the final report, it was alleged that the victim was aged only 9 years. When the accused were committed to trial on the final report being filed in the case, the Court of Session framed the following charges against them: First:- That, in the month of August 2012, from the kitchen of the house of the 2nd accused, having building number, Elamkunnapuzha gramapancyayat XVII/341, situated on the southern side of Beach Road, near to the Murikumpadam Belbo Street, Vypin-Munambam State Highway, you the 1st accused, with the knowledge and consent of the 2nd accused, had sexual intercourse with CW2, daughter of the 2nd accused, a girl child aged 9 years, three times repeatedly and, thereby you have committed the offence, rape, punishable u/s. 376 (2) (f) of IPC, within my cognizance.

Passage:
Raju Kumar Singh vs State on 20 May, 2016

IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI

RESERVED ON : MAY 16, 2016
DECIDED ON : MAY 20, 2016

Crl.A.1032/2015

RAJU KUMAR SINGH ..... Appellant

VERSUS

STATE ..... Respondent

CORAM:
HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE S.P.GARG

S.P.GARG, J.

1. The appellant Raju Kumar Singh has challenged the legality and correctness of a judgment dated 19.08.2015 of learned Additional Sessions Judge in Sessions Case No.72/14 arising out of FIR No.166/14 registered at Police Station Shalimar Bagh by which he was held guilty for committing offence punishable under Section 342 IPC and Sections 10 and 12 of POCSO Act. By an order dated 25.08.2015, he was sentenced to undergo Rigorous Imprisonment for five years with fine `5,000/- under Section 10 of POCSO Act; Rigorous Imprisonment for two months with fine `500/- under Section 342 IPC; and, Rigorous Imprisonment for one year with fine `1,000/- under Section 12 of POCSO AC. The sentences were to operate concurrently.
2. The incident whereby the child was sexually abused was reported to the police via Daily Diary (DD) No.87B (Ex.PW-3/A) on 21.02.2014 at around 10.50 p.m. The Investigating Officer after recording the statement of victim's mother (Ex.PW-4/A) lodged First Information Report. The prosecutrix was medically examined; she recorded her 164 Cr.P.C. statement. The accused was arrested. Statements of witnesses conversant with the facts were recorded. Upon completion of investigation, a charge-sheet was filed against the appellant for commission of various offences in the court. To substantiate its case, the prosecution examined 13 witnesses. The appellant denied his involvement in the crime and pleaded false implication; he did not produce any evidence in defence. The trial resulted in his conviction as aforesaid. Being aggrieved and dissatisfied, the instant appeal has been filed.
3. I have heard the learned counsel for the parties and have examined the file. The occurrence took place during daytime at around 3.30 p.m. when victim's mother Manju was away at her place of work. When she returned in the evening at around 8.30 p.m., the victim narrated the incident to her. She immediately went to the appellant's house but he was not present there. On his arrival at around 10.00-10.30 p.m., she confronted and slapped him. The incident was reported immediately to the police and DD No.87-B came into existence. In her statement (Ex.Pw-4/A), Manju gave a detailed account as to how and in what manner, her daughter 'X' (changed name) aged around five years was sexually assaulted by the appellant in her absence. The appellant was named in the FIR to be the perpetrator of the crime. In 164 Cr.P.C. statement (Ex.PW-2/F), the victim disclosed that the accused had taken her inside his room and had touched her private parts. She further disclosed that the accused had put off her underwear and had touched her private parts with tongue. In her 164 Cr.P.C. statement (Ex.PW-2/B), the complainant Manju also reiterated her version given to the police at first instance and implicated the accused to be the author of the crime.
4. Before recording her Court statement, the victim was asked various questions to find out if she was able to give rational answers to the questions put to her. The Presiding Officer after recording satisfaction that 'X' was competent to give her statement voluntarily, recorded her statement. She categorically attributed specific role to the accused in outraging her modesty in his house. She deposed that the accused had put off his underwear; he had also put off her underwear and touched her private parts; he had shown her nude photos on his mobile and had touched her private parts with his tongue. In the cross-examination, she disclosed that on the arrival of her mother, she apprised her about the occurrence. She further elaborated that the accused had done 'gandi baat' with her sister too and had shown her nude photos. She denied if the statement given by her in the court was tutored by her mother.
5. PW-4 (Manju), victim's mother corroborated her version in its entirety without any major variation. The statement of 'Y' (assumed name), victim's sister, was recorded as CW-1. In her statement 'Y' too deposed that the accused had taken her inside his house along with her sister 'X'; had shown her nude photos on his mobile; had disrobed her sister 'X'; and, had put off his underwear. In the cross-examination, she denied if she had given statement at the behest of her parents.
6. On scanning the testimonies of all these witnesses, it stands established that they are consistent throughout and despite cross-examination, nothing material could be extracted to disbelieve them. The witnesses had no ulterior motive to falsely implicate the accused for the heinous offence. They have corroborated each other without any inconsistencies and infirmities. The incident was reported without delay and the appellant was named to be the perpetrator of the crime. Specific and defined role was assigned to him. The accused taking advantage of the loneliness of the small children in the house had dared to outrage their modesty. It has come on record that victim's father Sunil was confined in Tihar Jail in a decoity case. Her mother used to go at her place of work during day time. It gave an opportunity to the appellant living in the neighborhood to exploit the innocence of the children. In the absence of any prior animosity or enmity, the victims and their mother are not expected to falsely implicate the appellant in a case of sexual abuse to bring themselves in disrepute. The defense put up by the accused deserves outright rejection. Nothing has come on record to show if on any particular date `10,000/- were borrowed by victim's father from him or that to avoid the said payment, the accused was falsely implicated in this case. For a trivial issue over return of money, the victim's parents are not expected to use their kids.
7. The Trial Court has discussed all the relevant aspects and the impugned order based upon fair appraisal of the evidence deserves no intervention. Sentence order is based upon fair reasoning and warrants no interference except that default sentence for non-payment of the fine shall be Simple Imprisonment for two months instead of nine months and fifteen days in all. Other terms and conditions of the sentence order are left undisturbed.
8. The appeal stands disposed of in the above terms. Trial Court record along with the copy of the order be sent back forthwith. Intimation be sent to Superintendent Jail.

Passage: Babu vs State Of Kerala on 29 November, 2017. IN THE HIGH COURT OF KERALA AT ERNAKULAM. PRESENT: THE HONOURABLE MR. JUSTICE RAJA VIJAYARAGHAVAN V. WEDNESDAY, THE 29TH DAY OF NOVEMBER 2017/8TH AGRAHAYANA, 1939. Bail Appl..No. 8184 of 2017. PETITIONER(S)/ACCUSED: BABU, AGED 52 YEARS, S/O.THAMPI, CHEMBAKASSERRY HOUSE, PULAYETTIKKARA, ARAYANKAVU, ERNAKULAM DISTRICT. RESPONDENT(S)/COMPLAINANT: STATE OF KERALA. THIS BAIL APPLICATION HAVING COME UP FOR ADMISSION ON 29-11-2017, THE COURT ON THE SAME DAY PASSED THE FOLLOWING: ORDER 1.This application is filed under Section 439 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. 2. The petitioner herein is the sole accused in Crime No.1336 of 2017 of Mulanthuruthy police station, registered alleging offence punishable under Sections 450, 376(2),(i),(n), 506 IPC and Section 3(d) r/w Sec. 4,5(1),(m),(n), r/w Sec.6 of POCSO Act, 2012. 3. The victim in the instant case is a minor girl aged 13 years. Her mother is no more and her father has remarried and is living elsewhere. She is now admitted in the Don Bosco Snehabhavan at Ernakulam. It appears that when she appeared moody and showed behavioral problems, a Counselor interacted with her. The disclosure she made is heart rending. The Counselor informed the child line authorities who in turn intimated the police. It is in the above fact scenario that the crime was registered. 4.According to the victim child, who is now studying in the 8th standard, the petitioner is a near relative and she calls him 'valiachan'. According to her, when she was in the 2nd standard, the petitioner started sexually assaulting her. He used to touch her private parts and used to perform oral sex on her. She was made to touch his genitals as well. On various occasions thereafter, the petitioner repeated the same acts. As she had no one to disclose the sexual abuse that she was being subjected to and as she was confused, she did not reveal this fact to anyone. In the month of September, 2017, the petitioner repeated the same acts. This tormented her and she became moody and restless which fact was noticed by the Counselor. Pursuant to the registration of the crime, the petitioner was arrested on 28.10.2017 and has been in custody since then. 5.The learned counsel appearing for the petitioner submits that the petitioner is a 52 year old man and is undergoing treatment for cardiac ailments at the Kottayam Medical College Hospital for the past six months. It is submitted that there are serious inconsistencies in the statement given by the victim child. There was no way that the petitioner could have sexually assaulted the minor child is the submission. 6.The learned Public Prosecutor has opposed the prayer and has made available the case diary for perusal. 7.I have gone through the same and It tells a very sad story. The victim child is a destitute with no one to look after and has been housed in various institutions since a very young age. The petitioner, by abusing his status as a near relative, has sexually assaulted the child. The investigation is still in the early stages and I find no reason to release the petitioner on bail. This application is dismissed.
Question: After reviewing the case details from Babu vs State of Kerala, what actions should the legal authorities prioritize regarding the protection of the minor victim, and what long-term legal remedies should be established within the existing child protection framework?
Correct Answer: Authorities should prioritize providing therapy and secure housing for the victim while ensuring legal proceedings against the accused are performed swiftly.
Reasoning: Given the victim's dire circumstances outlined in the passage, where she is without parental support and has faced significant trauma, the authorities must ensure her safety and well-being through therapy and stable housing while the legal process against the accused continues.

Passage:
Mr.X- Juvenile vs State Of U.P. And 3 Others on 4 April, 2023

JUDGMENT
HON'BLE SANJAY KUMAR PACHORI, J.
1. Heard Sri Shivam Yadav, Advocate holding brief of Sri Rajesh Yadav, learned counsel for the revisionist, Sri Ghanshyam Kesarwani, learned A.G.A. for the State and perused the material available on record. Despite service of notice upon opposite party No. 2, none appears on his behalf.
2. The Present Criminal Revision has been preferred by the revisionist through his mother under Section 102 of The Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015 (hereinafter referred to as "JJ Act, 2015") against the judgment dated 24.9.2022 passed by Special Judge (POCSO Act), Auraiya in Criminal Appeal No. 26 of 2022, whereby the appellate court has rejected the Criminal appeal and affirmed the order dated 29.8.2022 passed by Juvenile Justice Board, Auraiya. The Juvenile Justice Board has rejected the bail application of the revisionist, which has been filed by his natural guardian/mother, under Section 12 of "JJ Act, 2015" arising out of Case Crime No. 20 of 2022, under Sections 363, 366, 376, 504, 506 of the Indian Penal Code (in short "I.P.C.") and Section 3/4 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, Police Station- Bidhuna, District-  Auraiya.
3. Learned counsel for the revisionist submits that the revisionist is innocent and has been falsely implicated in the present case due to ulterior motive. The revisionist was 15 years, 1 month and 6 days old at the time of the incident. As per educational certificate, the Juvenile Justice Board declared the revisionist as juvenile vide order dated 29.8.2022 and no proceeding is pending against the aforesaid order. It is further submitted that the FIR has been lodged against the revisionist and three other named persons by father of the victim. It is further submitted that there is material contradictions in the statement of the victim recorded under Section 161 and 164 Cr.P.C. As per statement of the victim recorded under Section 164 Cr.P.C., the victim has gone to the house of her friend in Kanpur and the father of the victim lodged false FIR against the revisionist. It is further submitted that as per educational certificate, victim was 14 years and 5 months old at the time of incident. As per medical report, no external or internal injury has been found on the person of the victim. Clothes have been changed due to which the clothes have not been collected during the investigation. The revisionist is in protective custody in an observation home since 25.4.2022.
4. It is further submitted that the finding recorded by the court below is against the social information report and is based on surmises and conjectures. It has been further submitted that there is no evidence to show that if the revisionist is released on bail, his release is likely to bring him into association with any known criminal, or expose him to moral, physical, or psychological danger, or that his release would defeat the ends of justice. No such findings were recorded as to how he will come in contact with known criminals and how he will be exposed to moral, physical, or psychological danger, or that his release would defeat the ends of justice.
5. Learned counsel for the revisionist further submits that the revisionist has no criminal antecedent to his credit except the present case and is not a previous convict nor is he associated with any kind of unsocial or criminal activities. There is no report regarding any previous criminal antecedents of the family of the revisionist. The natural guardian/mother of the revisionist has given an undertaking that if the revisionist is released on bail, she will keep him in her custody and look after him properly and has assured on behalf of the juvenile that she is ready to cooperate with the process of law and shall faithfully make the juvenile available before the court whenever required and is also ready to accept all the conditions which the Court may deem fit.
6. It has been further submitted that the Juvenile Justice Board as well as the Appellate Court have not appreciated the Social Information Report of the Probation Officer in its right perspective and passed the impugned judgment and order in a cursory manner without considering the position of law and have declined bail to the revisionist. The bare perusal of the impugned orders demonstrates that the same has been passed on flimsy grounds, which have occasioned a gross miscarriage of justice. The judgment and order passed by the learned court below are illegal, contrary to law, and is based on the erroneous assumption of facts and law.
... [continues]

Correct Answer: The court carefully weighed the arguments, stressing that juvenile welfare must be prioritized even amid serious charges.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the court considered the submissions of the juvenile’s counsel, emphasizing the necessity of ensuring juvenile welfare and rights while also acknowledging the seriousness of the crimes charged, reflecting a balanced approach of the juvenile justice system.

Passage:
V.Vishnubhaskar ... Accused / vs The State - Rep. By on 16 November, 2017

This criminal appeal is against the conviction and sentence passed by the Mahila Judge, Coimbatore. The appellant / accused is convicted by the Trial Court for the offences punishable under Section 5 (l), (m), (n) and Section 6 of Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012 and sentenced to undergo life imprisonment and also to pay a fine of Rs.5,000/- and in default,to undergo rigorous imprisonment for a period of three months. The case of the prosecution as could be discerned from the evidence is as follows: The defacto complainant Nithya (PW1) is the wife of the appellant / accused Vishnubhaskar. The PW1 and the appellant were living separated for the last two years and Nithya (PW1) had left her girl child aged 3 years and 9 months with her parents living in Coimbatore, as she was employed in Bangalore. The victim child was unwell on 07.10.2015 and since PW1 was in Bangalore and could not make it, she had requested her husband (Accused) who was living in Coimbatore at Maniyakaranpalayam, Ganapathy, Coimbatore, to take the child to the hospital. The child was reportedly taken to Sri Ramakrishna Hospital and admitted for treatment and subsequently discharged on 08.10.2015. Further on 17.10.2015 and subsequent days the child was taken out by her father (Accused) at the behest of Nithya (PW1), during which time he committed penetrative sexual assault on his own child under the guise of checking her private part for any insect. The child had symptoms of restlessness, sleeplessness, frequent urination and itchy sensation in her private part and it was on 23.10.2015, that the child narrated her ordeal to Meenakshi (PW2) and to Shakunthala (PW4) who are the sister-in-law and mother of PW1 respectively. PW1 was informed immediately and she rushed to Coimbatore and took the child to the Sri Ramakrishna Hospital on 24.10.2015 at about 05.00 PM. Dr.Saranya Manickaraj (PW13) physically examined the child and her systemic examination report was marked as Ex.P14 in which it was mentioned as 'hymen intact' and no external injuries. Subsequently, the childline was informed by PW13 who in turn referred the child for psychological evaluation by “Vazikatti” a Non-Governmental Organization (NGO). PW1 also lodged a complaint (Ex.P1) with the Sub-Inspector of Police, All Women Police Station, Coimbatore and the same was registered as First Information Report (FIR) in Crime No.19/2015 by Tmt.Prema (PW14) Sub-Inspector of Police. Tmt.Rajeshwari (PW15) Inspector of Police referred the child for medical examination to the Government Hospital at Coimbatore on the same date. Dr.Sasikala (PW12) examined the child at about 10.15 PM on 24.10.2015 and found the hymen absent. She also found the vagina mucosa pinkish in colour and an abrasion of 0.5 cm on the right hand side of the private part of the child. A copy of Accident Register dated 24.10.2015 was marked as Ex.P13. Tmt.Rajeshwari (PW15) also recorded the statement of PW1 and PW4 on 24.10.2015 and on the next day i.e. on 25.10.2015, recorded the statements of PW2, PW5 & PW6 under Section 162 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. Thereafter, she arrested the accused, father of the victim child and prepared an observation Mahazar (Ex.P16) and also recorded the confession statement of the accused in the presence of Mohan Daniel (PW7) and Ramkumar (PW9). Tmt.Priyamalini (PW16) the Inspector of Police, All Women Police Station, Coimbatore who resumed duty after leave, took up the investigation and examined the child again on 26.10.2015. She also examined PW2 and took the child to the Judicial Magistrate III, Coimbatore for recording her statement under Section 164 of Criminal Procedure Code. On 06.11.2015, the accused was sent for potency test at Government Hospital, Coimbatore as per the order of Judicial Magistrate III, Coimbatore. Dr.Jayasingh (PW8) certified (Ex.P8) that there is nothing to suggest that the accused was impotent. This criminal appeal is against the conviction and sentence passed by the Mahila Judge, Coimbatore. The appellant / accused is convicted by the Trial Court for the offences punishable under Section 5 (l), (m), (n) and Section 6 of Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012 and sentenced to undergo life imprisonment and also to pay a fine of Rs.5,000/- and in default,to undergo rigorous imprisonment for a period of three months.
Question: In the context of the provided legal case summary, what are the legal consequences and remedies available to the child victim, given that her father has been convicted under the POCSO Act for aggravated sexual assault? What provisions in the law ensure her protection and what steps can be taken by the judicial system to safeguard her rights?
Correct Answer: The remedies available include compensation from the state for the crime committed, psychological evaluation, and protective measures under children's law, ensuring her safety from the perpetrator.
Reasoning: The passage outlines the conviction of the father under the POCSO Act, indicating that protections and remedies are available for the victim, including state compensation, psychological evaluations, and various support measures designed to ensure her long-term safety and well-being.

Passage: Nitin Singhal And Another vs State Of U.P. And Another on 12 January, 2018. The proceedings arise from serious allegations involving a minor victim who suffered from sexual abuse and exploitation by his tutor. The petitioners argued for dismissal of the case based on a settlement, but the court firmly stated that such grievous accusations fall outside the domain of simple disputes that can be settled privately. Utilizing precedents and established principles, the judgement firmly reinforced that heinous offenses involving minors under the POCSO Act must be prosecuted, reflecting a societal commitment to protecting children from such crimes. The court clearly outlined that the severity of the offense necessitated a thorough judicial inquiry, reaffirming public interest in deterring such acts.
Question: What does the court's refusal to quash the case based on settlement indicate about the nature of offenses committed against children?
Correct Answer: It signifies an understanding that offenses of this nature have a long-lasting impact on the victim and cannot be treated as private matters.
Reasoning: The judgement highlights the severe impact that such crimes have on victims and society as a whole, asserting that these offenses must be addressed in a court of law to protect future potential victims and ensure societal values are upheld.

Passage: Pardeep Kumar @ Deepa vs State Of Punjab on 15 October, 2018. In the High Court of Punjab and Haryana, the petitioner sought regular bail under Section 439 Cr.P.C. in case FIR No.273 dated 06.11.2016, under Section 377 of IPC and Section 4 of POCSO Act. The petitioner has been in custody since November 2016 for allegedly sexually abusing a 9-year-old child. The learned counsel argued the request for bail given the time taken to complete trial proceedings and no other cases against the petitioner. However, the State counsel emphasized the seriousness of the charges and the evidence of abuse. The court noted the petitioner's threats to the child and dismissed the bail application. The trial court was directed to expedite the trial proceedings.
Question: Given the details of the case mentioned, what steps can the trial court take to ensure justice for the minor child involved, and what legal protections are available under Indian law for child victims of sexual offenses?
Correct Answer: The trial court can expedite the proceedings and ensure the child receives counseling and support services after the trial.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the trial court was directed to expedite proceedings due to the gravity of the offenses against the child. Indian law provides various protections for child victims such as the POCSO Act, which emphasizes the need for quick trial processes, and ensuring that victims are provided with support services to aid their recovery.

Passage: IN THE HIGH COURT OF KERALA AT ERNAKULAM PRESENT: THE HONOURABLE MR. JUSTICE SUNIL THOMAS MONDAY, THE 25TH DAY OF JUNE 2018 / 4TH ASHADHA, 1940 Bail Appl..No. 4054 of 2018 (CRIME NO. 548/2018 OF OTTAPALAM POLICE STATION , PALAKKAD) PETITIONER(S)/ACCUSED 1 SANAL KUMAR K., AGED 36 YEARS, S/O.K.M.UNNIKRISHNAN, ANASWARA, KOOTHAKKURISSY, PANAMANNA P.O., OTTAPPALAM, PALAKKAD DISTRICT-679 501. 2 UNNIKRISHNAN K.M., AGED 67 YEARS, ANASWARA, KOOTHAKKURISSY, PANAMANNA P.O., OTTAPPALAM, PALAKKAD DISTRICT-679 501. 3 RADHA, W/O.UNNIKRISHNAN K.M., ANASWARA, KOOTHAKKURISSY, PANAMANNA P.O., OTTAPPALAM, PALAKKAD DISTRICT-679 501. BY ADV.SRI.M.T.SURESHKUMAR RESPONDENT(S)/COMPLAINANT: STATE OF KERALA, REPRESENTED BY PUBLIC PROSECUTOR, HIGH COURT OF KERALA, ERNAKULAM-682031. R BY PUBLIC PROSECUTOR SMT.V.SREEJA THIS BAIL APPLICATION HAVING BEEN FINALLY HEARD ON 19-06-2018, THE COURT ON 25/6/2018 PASSED THE FOLLOWING: ORDER The accused in Crime No. 548/2018 of Ottappalam Police station for offences punishable under sections 7 & 8 of POCSO Act 2012 and under section 75 of the Juvenile Justice Care and Protection of Children Act 2015, are the petitioners herein. Admitted facts are as follows; The first petitioner had married the defacto complainant on 27/4/2009. Thereafter, they resided together for some time. The second petitioner is his father and the third petitioner is his mother. A girl child was born, who is aged 7 years now. Matrimonial dispute arose between the parties. It is stated that, on 5/9/2017, the wife left the matrimonial home. The child was in the custody of the petitioners till 15/9/2017, on which day the child was entrusted with the parents of the defacto complainant. The present complaint was laid by the wife alleging that the first petitioner had sexually abused the seven year old girl on few days between 5/9/2017 to 15/9/2017 at the matrimonial home and other places. Apprehending arrest, the petitioners herein have approached the court. The specific contention of the petitioners herein is that, the defacto complainant was a quarrelsome person and matrimonial relationship became incompatible. It was alleged that, the defacto complainant had developed a close intimacy with one doctor who was younger to her. She was found making secret audio and video calls to him over the mobile phone. When the relationship went beyond a limit, the husband intervened and she was warned. It was later understood that she continued to maintain even physical relationship with him. On being confronted with materials, she is stated to have abandoned the child and left the matrimonial home on 5/9/2017. Thereafter, the first petitioner herein entrusted the child to the defacto complainant and her parents, considering the education of the child. The first petitioner herein initiated matrimonial proceedings, including an application for custody of the child. The present complaint was filed belatedly raising absolutely false allegations with the intention of denying him the custody of the child. It was vehemently contended that, the first petitioner herein has not committed any sexual abuse on the minor girl and it was purposefully and belatedly set up. Annexure A1 is a copy of the petition in O.P.(HMA) No. 649/2017 dated 27/10/2017 filed by the first petitioner herein before the Family Court, Malappuram. He claimed divorce on the ground of adultery of the defacto complainant with the doctor, who was arrayed as the second respondent in that O.P. It was alleged therein that, she was having continuous physical relationship with that doctor and was caught with evidence. Annexure A2 is a copy of O.P.( G & W) No. 356/2018 filed by the first petitioner herein on 24/5/2018 before the Family Court, Malappuram claiming custody of the child. Thereafter,Crime was got registered by the wife at Bangalore as Crime No. 703/2017 on 11/12/2017 against the petitioners herein alleging matrimonial cruelty. The first petitioner sought bail which was granted by Annexure A3 order. It is seen that, she alleged that the petitioners herein committed acts of matrimonial cruelty and demanded dowry and thereby committed offences punishable under section 498A IPC and the provisions of the Dowry Prohibition Act. The petitioner was granted anticipatory bail. Annexure A5 is a complaint filed by the defacto complainant before the State Commission for Protection of Child Child Rights of Kerala and Karnataka, with copies to the various authorities. It was alleged that, the father-in-law of the defacto complainant had made sexual advances towards her. She alleged that, in-laws had administered various medicines on her body. It was also alleged that, the child was physically assaulted by the petitioners during the period from 5/9/2017 to 15/9/2017. There is an allegation that, the grandfather used to beat her on her groin and grand mother had beaten the child with a chapathy rolling pin. Annexure A6 is the complaint No.20/2018 filed on 24/1/2018 by the defacto complainant under the provisions of the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act. The above documents clearly show that, at no point of time earlier, the defacto complainant had a case that the child was sexually abused by the father. Even though physical assault is alleged against the petitioners, there was absolutely no reference anywhere that the child was subjected to sexual abuse by the father. It seems that, for the first time such a complaint is seen raised in the present crime(Crime No.548/2018 of Ottappalam Police station). Crime was thereupon registered for offences punishable under sections 7 & 8 of POCSO Act and Section 75 of the Juvenile Justice Care and Protection of Children Act 2015. The crux of the allegation in the FIS is that, during the period when the child was in the custody of the petitioners, the father of the child used to give her a bath. He used to undress her, remove his dress and give her bath. It was stated that in the course of bathing her, the first petitioner used to tickle her. The child has stated that, she did not like boys giving her bath. Pursuant to it, section 164 Cr.P.C.statement of the victim was also recorded, which is substantially in tune with FI statement. It is pertinent to note that the only allegation raised against the first petitioner is as above. Even if the entire statement made by the child is taken at its face value, crucial question that arises is whether the father had any sexual intent while giving the daughter a bath. This has to be appreciated in the background that the allegation comes only at a highly belated stage, when the father himself had sought custody of the child. No explanation is forthcoming for the delay in giving complaint. Hence, the question to be investigated by the police is only whether the first petitioner herein with sexual intention had committed any act. For that, custodial interrogation does not appear to be warranted. It is also clear that, in such a very strained matrimonial relationship a belated allegation of this nature has to be considered carefully and it may not be safe to order custodial interrogation. Having regard to the above, I am inclined to grant benefit of anticipatory bail to the petitioners herein subject to the following conditions: i). Petitioners shall appear before the investigating officer within ten days from today and shall undergo interrogation. Thereafter, if they are proposed to be arrested, they shall be released on bail on each of the petitioner executing a bond for a sum of Rs.50,000/-(Rupees fifty thousand only) with two sureties for the like sum each. ii). They shall appear before the Investigating Officer as and when called for and co-operate with the investigation. Anticipatory Bail Application is allowed.
Question: In the legal context of the passage described, what steps can be legally taken by the defacto complainant after filing the initial complaint regarding the child abuse under the POCSO Act?
Correct Answer: The defacto complainant can seek protection orders and request for the child's custody during the legal proceedings.
Reasoning: Given that the passage revolves around serious allegations pertaining to child abuse under the POCSO Act, the defacto complainant has the right to seek legal protection measures such as obtaining protection orders and ensuring the child's safety, along with requesting custody while the case is ongoing.

NO.171/2023 BY ALLOWING THE PETITION IN THE INTEREST OF JUSTICE AND EQUITY. THIS PETITION IS COMING ON FOR ORDERS, THIS DAY, THE COURT MADE THE FOLLOWING: CORAM: THE HON'BLE SMT. JUSTICE LALITHA KANNEGANTI ORAL ORDER Aggrieved by the order passed in Crl.Misc.No.171/2023 dated 02.02.2024 by the Principal Judge, Family Court, Gadag, the petitioner/husband is before this Court. 2. The parties are referred to as husband and wife for the sake of convenience. 3. The wife had filed Crl.Misc.No.171/2023 seeking maintenance of an amount of Rs.30,000/- per month and litigation expenses of an amount of Rs.50,000/-. It is the case of the wife that she has been ill-treated by the husband and his family members have abused and assaulted her. Out of the wedlock, she gave birth to one male child and due to ill health, the said child died. It is her case that the husband is running a Chicken shop and he is getting daily income of an amount of Rs.15,000/- to Rs.20,000/- and he is getting income of an amount of Rs.5 to 8 Lakhs from his land. It is stated that the wife has no source of income and she is not in a position to maintain herself. 4. The trial Court has given a finding that the husband had neglected the wife, when it comes to the quantum of maintenance, the trial Court observed that the wife has claimed maintenance of an amount of Rs.30,000/- per month, which is exorbitant one and it is prima facie found that the cause of justice would be served if the wife is granted reasonable maintenance by keeping in mind the inflation and high cost of living. The trial Court observed that in the process of doing complete justice between the parties, the trial Court had granted an amount of Rs.4,000/- per month. Aggrieved thereby, the husband is before this Court. 5. Learned counsel appearing for the petitioner/husband submits that the amount that is granted by the trial Court is on the higher side and it is an ex-parte order and without any basis, such order is passed. He submits that the wife is not entitled for maintenance as she neglected the husband. As such, the order impugned is liable to be set aside. 6. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent/wife submits that the trial Court had granted only an amount of Rs.4,000/- per month as maintenance and even that is also questioned by the husband. It is submitted that when the husband has received the notice from the Court, he had avoided intentionally and he is coming up before this Court with different stands and this petition is liable to be dismissed. 7. Having heard the learned counsels on either side, perused the entire material on record. The wife had claimed maintenance of an amount of Rs.30,000/- per month and litigation expenses of an amount of Rs.50,000/-. The trial Court had granted an amount of Rs.4,000/- per month as maintenance and an amount of Rs.10,000/- as litigation expenses. With regard to the ground that is raised by the husband that it is an ex-parte order, why he has not appeared before the Court when he received the notices, no positive explanation is coming forth. Neither he has appeared before the Court nor he has filed his statement of assets and liabilities before this Court to show that he is not in a position to pay even an amount of Rs.4,000/- per month to the wife. In that view of the matter, this Court finds no reason to interfere with the order passed by the trial Court. Hence, the following: ORDER i. Accordingly, the revision petition is dismissed. ii. As per the order passed by this Court, Demand Draft is deposited by the petitioner before this Court and the respondent is at liberty to withdraw the amount. iii. All I.As. in this petition shall stand closed. Sd/- JUSTICE LALITHA KANNEGANTI

Correct Answer: She can file for a restraining order against her husband to prevent further abuse.
Reasoning: Given the wife's claims of abuse and ill-treatment, in addition to seeking maintenance, she can also pursue a restraining order to legally protect herself from the husband and prevent further incidents of abuse.

Passage: Madhya Pradesh High Court Nikhil Sarathe vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh on 28 August, 2018 1 M.Cr.C.No.16856 of 2017 HIGH COURT OF MADHYA PRADESH PRINCIPAL SEAT AT JABALPUR Single Bench : Hon'ble Shri Rajeev Kumar Dubey, J. Misc. Criminal Case No.16856/2017 Nikhil Sarathe vs. State of M.P. and Others -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Shri Alok Bagrecha and Shri Pushpendra Dubey, learned counsel for the applicant. Shri Manish Awasthi, Govt. Advocate for the respondent/State. Shri Vivek Agrawal, learned counsel for the respondent no.2/prosecutrix. None for the respondent No.3/complainant though served. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ORDER Reserved on : 21/08/2018 Delivered on : /08/2018 This petition has been filed under Section 482 of Cr.P.C. seeking quashing of the F.I.R. of crime No.293/2016 registered at PS Kotwali Bhopal for the offence punishable under Sections 363, 376, 120-B, 212 of IPC and Section 3, 4 of POCSO Act and consequential proceedings thereto. Brief facts of the case are that on 29/12/2016, the father of the prosecutrix lodged a report at P.S. Kotwali Bhopal averring that his daughter (prosecutrix) age about 17 years was not at home since six 'o'clock in the morning and she was not found even after enough search. Someone had abducted her. On that police registered crime No. 293/16 for the offence punishable under section 363 of I.P.C. and investigated the matter and during investigation on 23/01/17 police rescued the prosecutrix from the custody of applicant and recorded the statement of the prosecutrix. In her statement prosecutrix stated that she had known applicant Nikhil for the last five years and there was a love affair between them. On 29th December 2016, Nikhil had asked him to escape from the house in order to marry with him. On this, around 5-6 o'clock in the morning without informing her family members she went to the Hawa Mahal, where she met Nikhil who took her to Arya Samaj Mandir situated at Nehru Nagar, where both of them got married, then they went to Shirdi where they stayed for four or five days. Thereafter they came back to Bhopal and stayed there for 5-6 days at Nikhil's friend's house situated at Neelbad area in Bhopal. During that period Nikhil also made physical relationship with her, after which Nikhil's friend Shubham (co-accused) took them to Jabalpur, where they stayed in the house of a person, who happened to be an acquaintance of Shubham, as a guest for 6-7 days. There too applicant Nikhil Had sex with her four to five times. On that police also added offences under section 368, 376, 120-B, 212 of IPC and Section 3, 4,5,6,11 (i) and 12 of POCSO Act and after investigation of the crime police filed charge-sheet against the applicant. On that charge-sheet S.T. No. 28/18 was registered. Applicant is facing trial in that case before 7 th A.S.J. Bhopal and filed this petition for quashing the F.I.R. of the crime No. 293/16 registered at P.S. Kotwali Bhopal and the proceedings of S.T. No. 28/18. Learned counsel for the applicant submitted that the prosecutrix was major at the time of incident and went with the applicant on her own will. In the birth certificate of the prosecutrix, her date of birth is mentioned as 30/11/1998. According to that certificate, prosecutrix was major at the time of incident. The prosecutrix in her statement recorded by the JMFC under Section 164 of Cr.P.C. also deposed that she went with the applicant at her own will and she had married with applicant and is living with him. Since prosecutrix was major at the time of incident and went with applicant own her on will, no offences under Section 363, 376, 120-B, 212 of IPC and Section 3, 4 of POCSO Act is made out against the applicant. He further submitted that if it is assumed that the age of the prosecutrix was 17 years two months at the time of incident as appears on the basis of school entry register produced by the prosecution along with the charge sheet even then no offence is made out against the applicant from the charge-sheet because prosecutrix went with the applicant on her own will. In this regard, he also placed reliance on Delhi High Court judgment passed in Laxmi Devi & Anr.; Maha Dev & Devender @ Babli v. State reported in (2012) DLT 619 and Apex Court judgment passed in Satish Mehra Vs. State reported in 2013 CrLJ 411, Rajeev Thaper Vs. Madan Lal reported in (2013) 3 SCC 330 and Independent thought Vs. Union of India reported in (2017) 10 SCC 800, and State of Karnataka Vs. L. Muniswamy and others reported in 1977 CRLJ 1125. Learned counsel for the prosecutrix also submitted that prosecutrix was major at the time of incident and went with the applicant on her own will and at present she is living with the applicant and also married with the him, so offence registered against the applicant should be quashed. Learned counsel for the State opposed the prayer and submitted that according to school entry register the date of birth of the prosecutrix is 23/10/1999 and on that basis the age of the prosecutrix was below 18 years at the time of incident i.e. 29/12/16 and she was minor at the time of incident. Applicant abducted the prosecutrix and made sexual relations with her, so offence under Section 368, 376 of IPC and Section 3/ 4 and 12 of POCSO is clearly made out against the applicant and prays for rejection of petition. This Court has gone through the record and arguments put forth by the learned counsel for both the parties. In the entry of school register, the date of prosecutrix is mentioned 23/10/1999 and on that basis the age of the prosecutrix appears to be below 18 years at the time of incident i.e. 29/12/16 and she was minor at the time of incident. Applicant took the prosecutrix and made sexual relations with her. Although applicant produced Birth certificate of the prosecutrix in which the date of birth of prosecutrix is mentioned as 30/11/1998 but authenticity of that certificate has to be tested and it requires evidence to decide. So at this stage only on that basis it can not be said that prosecutrix was major at the time of incident. Fact of the cases relied on by the learned counsel of the applicant do not match with the instant case. The case Laxmi Devi & Anr.; Maha Dev & Devender @ Babli v. State (supra) relate to the incidents that occurred before November 2012 when POCSO Act was not in force. While this incident occurred on 29/12/2016 when POCSO Act had come into force and according to section 3 of the POCSO Act intercourse with a woman below 18 years of age is the offence irrespective of the fact that she is a wife of accused. in the case of Satish Mehra v. State (NCT of Delhi), (Supra) apex court held "charge sheet only quashed if the attending facts and circumstances satisfy the narrow test that even accepting all the allegations levelled by the prosecution, no offence is disclosed. While in this case offence under POCSO Act is prima facie made out against applicant. Because on the basis of school entry register and the statement of parents of the prosecutrix her age appeared to be below 18 years. In the case of Rajeev Thaper Vs. Madan Lal (supra) apex court held "To invoke its inherent jurisdiction under Section 482 CrPC, the High Court has to be fully satisfied that the material produced by the accused is such that would lead to the conclusion that his/their defence is based on sound, reasonable, and indubitable facts; but the material relied upon by the accused should be of sterling and impeccable quality." While in this case the authenticity of birth certificate of the prosecutrix has to be proved which requires evidence. On the other hand prosecution produced entry of school admission register to prove the age of prosecutrix in which date of birth of prosecutrix is mentioned as 23/10/1999. In the case of Independent thought Vs. Union of India (Supra) apex court held that Exception 2 to Section 375 I.P.C. in so far as it relates to a girl child below 18 years is liable to be struck down on the following grounds:- (i) it is arbitrary, capricious, whimsical and violative of the rights of the girl child and not fair, just and reasonable and, therefore, violative of Article 14, 15 and 21 of the Constitution of India. In the case of State of Karnataka Vs. L. Muniswamy and others (supra) Apex court held that High Court is entitled to quash a proceeding if it comes to conclusion that allowing the proceeding to continue would be an abuse of the process of the court or that the ends of the justice requires that the preceding out to quashed. While in this case if it is proved that prosecutrix was below 18 years of age at the time of incident, there is sufficient evidence to proceed against the applicant in the case. So these judgements do not help applicant much. Hon'ble Supreme Court in the case of Yogendra Yadav and others vs. State of Jharkhand and another (2014) 9 SCC 653 held that offences which involve moral turpitude, grave offences like rape murder cannot be effaced by quashing proceedings under section 482 because they have harmful effect on society and are not restricted to two individuals or group quashing of such offences may send a wrong signal to society. In the case in hand prosecution produced the entry of school register along with charge sheet in which date of birth of prosecutrix is mentioned 23/10/1999 and on that basis the age of the prosecutrix appears to be below 18 years at the time of incident i.e. 29/12/16 and she was minor at the time of incident. Applicant took the prosecutrix along with him and made sexual relations with her. So from the charge sheet offence under section 3/4 of the POCSO Act is prima facie made out against the applicant. So FIR and the proceedings of S.T. No. 28/18 can not be quashed at this stage. Although applicant produced Birth certificate of the prosecutrix in which the date of birth of prosecutrix is mentioned as 30/11/1998 but authenticity of that certificate has to be tested and it requires evidence to decide. Applicant is free to raise this issue before trial court at the appropriate stage and produce the evidence in support of his defence which will consider by the trial court on merits. Accordingly, petition is disposed of. (Rajeev Kumar Dubey) Judge vs Digitally signed by VARSHA SINGH Date: 2018.08.29 17:55:33 +05'30'
Question: In the case of Nikhil Sarathe vs. The State of Madhya Pradesh, what legal implications arise regarding the prosecutrix's age and consent according to the POCSO Act based on the information presented in the passage? Specifically, how does the contention regarding the prosecutrix being a minor at the time of the incident affect the legal standing of the charges against Nikhil Sarathe?
Correct Answer: The charges under the POCSO Act remain valid as the prosecutrix was below 18 years of age.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the prosecutrix's birth date, according to the school register, is 23/10/1999, making her a minor at the time of the incident on 29/12/2016. The POCSO Act strictly prohibits sexual relations with a minor, regardless of what may be argued regarding consent, thereby affirming that the charges against Nikhil Sarathe are valid.

Passage: This Criminal Original Petition has been filed to quash the proceedings in Spl.S.C. No.126 of 2024 on the file of the learned Sessions Judge, Special Court for POCSO Act, Madurai. The case of the prosecution is that the petitioner had married the third respondent/victim girl and had a physical relationship with her. The victim had given birth on 20.09.2022. A complaint was lodged by the Development Officer. Both parties later presented a joint Memo of Compromise indicating that they had settled their dispute amicably, and the victim intends to withdraw her complaint. The Court stated that while it has the power to quash criminal proceedings under certain conditions, it should do so cautiously. Passage: Hamza vs State Of Kerala on 31 August, 2017. IN THE HIGH COURT OF KERALA AT ERNAKULAM PRESENT: THE HONOURABLE MR. JUSTICE P.UBAID TUESDAY, THE 17TH DAY OF OCTOBER 2017/25TH ASWINA, 1939 Bail Appl..No. 7052 of 2017 () CRMC 1066/2017 of SPECIAL COURT FOR THE TRIAL OF OFFENCES AGAINST CHILDREN (ADDITIONAL SESSIONS COURT-I), MANJERI DATED 31.8.2017 CRIME NO.192/2017 OF EDAKKARA POLICE STATION, MALAPPURAM. PETITIONER(S)/ACCUSED : HAMZA, AGED 42 YEARS, S/O. MOIDEENKUTTY, CHAPPATHODIKA HOUSE, PALLIPADI PALEMADU P.O., EDAKKARA, MALAPPURAM DISTRICT. BY ADVS.SRI.P.VENUGOPAL SMT.T.J.MARIA GORETTI RESPONDENT(S)/COMPLAINANT : STATE OF KERALA REPRESENTED BY PUBLIC PROSECUTOR, HIGH COURT OF KERALA, ERNAKULAM - 682 031 (REPRESENTING S.H.O EDAKKARA POLICE STATION IN CRIME NO. 192/2017). BY PUBLIC PROSECUTOR SRI.SAJJU.S. THIS BAIL APPLICATION HAVING COME UP FOR ADMISSION ON 17-10-2017, THE COURT ON THE SAME DAY PASSED THE FOLLOWING: O R D E R The petitioner herein is the accused in Crime No.192/2017 of Edakkara Police Station. He seeks pre arrest bail under Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure on the apprehension of arrest and custodial harassment in connection with the said crime registered under Sections 7 & 8 of the POCSO Act and Section 5 of the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015. The victim of offence in this case is a boy aged 17 years. His complaint is that on a day some time prior to 26.7.2017, the petitioner herein pressed on his penis, and thus sexually abused him. The complaint does not contain the date of the alleged incident. The complaint was filed by the victim on 26.7.2017, on the intervention of the child line people. The prosecution records would show that when examined under Section 164 Cr.P.C., the victim stated the date of incident as 22.6.2017. The complaint does not contain any reason for the delay in making the complaint. It appears that the victim made the complaint at the instance of somebody. The petitioner's case is that the present complaint is the result of some other animosity. Let the factual aspects be probed into by the trial court during trial. In the present circumstances, where things are slightly doubtful I feel it appropriate to grant pre arrest bail to the accused. It will suffice that the petitioner is directed to report before the Investigating Officer for necessary interrogation. In the result, this petition for pre arrest bail is allowed. The petitioner is ordered to be released on bail on his executing a bond with two solvent sureties for 40,000/- (Rupees Forty thousand only) each to the satisfaction of the arresting officer or the court below having jurisdiction, in case of his arrest in connection with Crime No.192/2017 of Edakkara Police Station. Bail is granted on condition that; a) The petitioner shall report before the Investigating Officer between 10 a.m. and 11 a.m. on all Mondays for a period of two months. b) The petitioner shall not leave the limits of Edakkara Police Station for a period of two months. c) The petitioner shall not in any manner influence or intimidate the material witnesses, or he shall not have any contact with the material witnesses directly or over telephone or otherwise.

Passage: IN THE HIGH COURT OF KERALA AT ERNAKULAM PRESENT THE HONOURABLE DR. JUSTICE KAUSER EDAPPAGATH Friday, the 18th day of February 2022 / 29th Magha, 1943 CRL.M.APPL.NO.1/2020 IN CRL.A NO. 437 OF 2020 SC 102/2015 OF THE ADDITIONAL SESSIONS COURT - I , KOTTAYAM PETITIONER/APPELLANT: THOMAS @ JOSHY, AGED 37 YEARS, S/O. THOMMY, PUTHENPURAKAL VEEDU, NAKRAL, PUTHUVAL BHAGOM, PERUNNA WEST KARA, CHANGANACHERRY. RESPONDENT/RESPONDENT: STATE OF KERALA, REP. BY PUBLIC PROSECUTOR, HIGH COURT OF KERALA, ERNAKULAM 682 031. Application praying that in the circumstances stated therein the High Court be pleased to suspend the execution of the sentence imposed on the appellant in the judgment dated 18.03.2020 in S.C.No. 102/2015 of the Additional Sessions Judge-I, Kottayam pending disposal of this appeal and to grant bail to the petitioner/appellant. This Application coming on for orders upon perusing the application and upon hearing the arguments of GEORGE SEBASTIAN, SRI.BOBBY JOHN, Advocates for the petitioner and of the PUBLIC PROSECUTOR for the respondent, the court passed the following: ORDER Crl.M.A.No.1/2020 This is an application filed to suspend the execution of sentence. The petitioner/appellant has been convicted for the offences punishable under Sections 377, 376(2)(f), 376(2)(i), 376(2)(j) and 376(2)(k), 376(2)(n) of IPC and Section 6 read with 5(l), 5(m) and 5(n) of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act (POCSO Act for short). He is sentenced to undergo rigorous imprisonment for 10 years and to pay a fine of Rs.30,000/- for the offence punishable under Section 6 of the POCSO Act. Separate punishment is not ordered under Sections 376(2)(f), 376(2)(i), 376(2)(j) and 376(2)(k) and 376(2)(n) of IPC as provided in Section 42 of the POCSO Act. I have heard Sri.. George Sebastian, the learned counsel for the petitioner and Smt. Sheeba Thomas, the learned Public Prosecutor. The victim is none other than the daughter of the petitioner. The incident had taken place when the victim was only 10 months of age. The petitioner who should have been the protector of the child as a father, turned out to be a demon and sexually abused his own child. I went through the relevant portion of the judgment. Paragraph 12 of the judgment deals with the evidence of PW1 who is the mother of the victim and wife of the petitioner. She specifically deposed that the incident had taken place on 24/1/2015 and 25/1/2015. According to her, she saw traces of semen on the hip and corner of the mouth of the victim, who was only 7 months age at that time. She further deposed that on seeing this, she searched the accused and he was found lying naked on the bed. She further deposed that on the next day she witnessed the accused putting his sexual organ into the mouth of the victim and inserting his finger into her vagina. PW3 is the doctor. Ext.P2 is the wound certificate. Paragraph 24 of the judgment deals with the evidence of the doctor. The doctor deposed that she examined the victim and issued Ext.P2 wound certificate. According to the doctor, PW1 narrated the history of rape, oral sex and putting fingers into the vagina of the victim by the petitioner. The doctor further deposed that on examination, the hymen of the victim was found absent. I am not commenting on these evidence at this stage for the reason that it has to be considered at the time of final hearing. However, the fact remains that the court below believed the testimony of PW1 as well as PW3. The learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that there is seven days' delay in registering the FIR and no independent witnesses were examined. The learned counsel further submitted that there was marital dispute between the petitioner and PW1 and hence the evidence of PW1 could not have been relied on. I cannot rely on those submissions as such. Those contentions can be raised at the time of final hearing. Considering the heinous nature of crime committed by the petitioner, I am of the view that his sentence cannot be suspended. Accordingly, this application is dismissed.

Passage: The case Ashok vs Public Health Department illustrates the legal mechanisms available for minors seeking to terminate pregnancies resulting from sexual abuse. The petitioner, a 17-year-old girl, filed for the termination of her pregnancy through a writ petition, emphasizing her right under the Constitution of India to live with dignity and liberty. The court emphasized the importance of medical evaluations by the committee, which supported the termination based on the mental health impacts of unwanted pregnancy due to rape. The passage outlines the procedures that need to be followed under the Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act, highlighting the necessity of obtaining consent from guardians for minors, underlining the importance of safeguarding the rights and well-being of young victims.
Question: What legal framework governs the termination of pregnancy for minors in the context of Ashok vs Public Health Department, particularly with respect to informed consent and mental health considerations?
Correct Answer: Termination is only possible if minors have written consent from a guardian and medical practitioners acknowledge mental health risks.
Reasoning: The passage makes it clear that for minors, consent from a guardian is required for the termination of pregnancy unless immediate medical intervention is necessary, aligning with the legal rights of minors and the stipulations of the Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act.
