Passage:
Akhtar Khan vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh on 14 July, 2023

Madhya Pradesh High Court
Akhtar Khan vs The State Of Madhya Pradesh on 14 July, 2023
Author: Rohit Arya
Bench: Rohit Arya
1
IN THE HIGH COURT OF MADHYA PRADESH AT GWALIOR CRA No. 1152 of 2023 (AKHTAR KHAN Vs THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH AND OTHERS)
Dated : 14-07-2023 Shri F.A. Shah- Advocate for the appellant. Dr. Anjali Gyanani - Public Prosecutor for respondent/State. Heard on I.A. No.7003 of 2023, which is first application under Section 389(1) Cr.P.C. filed on behalf of appellant-Akhtar Khan seeking suspension of sentence and grant of bail. Appellant-Akhtar Khan stood convicted under Section 4(2) of POCSO Act and sentenced to undergo 20 years' R.I. with a fine of Rs.2,000/- and under Section 9 of Prohibition of Child Marriage Act and sentenced to undergo 2 years' R.I with a fine of Rs.1,000/- with default stipulations vide judgment of conviction and order of sentence dated 15/12/2022 passed by Special Judge (POCSO Act), Shivpuri (M.P.) in Special Case No.124/2019. Present appellant so far has undergone incarceration of about 17 months in toto, as transpired during the course of hearing. Co-accused/Appellant No.1 in Criminal Appeal No.838 of 2023-Shakil Khan is the biological father of the prosecutrix as after the demise of his first wife and mother of the prosecutrix, he married to co-accused Shehnaz Bano. As per prosecution story, the prosecutrix claiming herself to be 14 years of age, on 20/09/2019, lodged an FIR to the effect that for the last 4-5 years since the time her mother expired, she was ill-treated by her step-mother Shehnaz Bano off and on. She, therefore, had left the house and started living with her grand-parents. Thereafter, on 1/1/2019, while she was staying with her maternal uncle's house, step-mother Shehnaz came there and against her wishes brought her back. Thereafter, she started searching for the match though she was not major. She manipulated her marriage (Nikah) with accused/appellant Akhtar and Farukh Khan had reduced in writing the process of Nikah. After so-called solemnization of marriage (Nikah), she was subjected to forcible sexual abuse against her wishes by Akhtar from 24/08/2019 to 28/08/2019. Thereafter, accused/appellant Akhtar called her grand-parents to the Kota Court and whereafter, signing of certain documents, she was given in custody of her grand-parents. Thereafter, she came back to her grand-parents' house from Kota. It is alleged that appellant Akhtar had also threatened the prosecutrix with dire consequences, if she divulged the factum of marriage and that of sexual abuse. In the aforesaid backdrop, the prosecutrix filed the complaint and the same was registered at Crime No.213/19. Based thereupon, investigation was set in motion. The Investigating Officer, upon collection of incriminating evidence, filed challan. The Special Court upon critical evaluation of the evidence placed on record convicted and sentenced the present appellant as aforesaid. Learned counsel for the appellant while taking exception to the impugned judgement submits that the prosecutrix in her statement before the Court has clearly stated that her date of birth is 11/10/2000 as recorded in Aadhar Card and therefore, she was major as on the date of incident. However, the trial Court in Para-20 of the judgement, ignored the same and relied upon her date of birth as per the certificate of the Municipal Corporation, Kota (Ex.P/10) as 11/10/2005. That apart, she has turned hostile and belied the story of prosecution. Shakil Khan, co-convict/appellant No.1 in Criminal Appeal No.838 of 2023, has already been admitted to suspension of sentence and grant of bail vide order dated 27.04.2023. On such grounds, learned counsel prays for suspension of sentence and grant of bail to the present appellant. Per contra, learned Public Prosecutor for respondent/State, while opposing the application supporting the judgment impugned submits that looking to the gravity of offence, no exception can be taken in the matter of suspension of sentence and grant of bail to the present appellant. Upon hearing learned counsel for the parties, though this Court refrains from commenting upon rival contentions touching merits of the case but regard being had to the fact that co-convict/appellant No.1 -Shakil Khan in Criminal Appeal No.838 of 2023 has already been admitted to suspension of sentence and grant of bail vide order dated 27.04.2023, the present appellant has so far undergone incarceration of 17 months in toto coupled with the fact that appeal is of the year 2023 and there is no likelihood of its early hearing in near future and therefore, in the obtaining facts and circumstances, we hereby extend the benefit of suspension of sentence and grant of bail to present appellant-Akhtar Khan. Accordingly, I.A.7003 of 2023 stands allowed and it is directed that the jail sentence of appellant-Akhtar Khan shall remain suspended during pendency of present appeal and he shall be released on bail, subject to verification of amount of fine being deposited and on his furnishing a personal bond in the sum of Rs.2,00,000/- (Rupees Two Lacs only) with one solvent surety in the like amount to the satisfaction of the Trial Court. Appellant-Akhtar Khan is directed to appear before the Registry of this Court first on 13.09.2023 and on other subsequent dates as may be fixed by Office in this behalf. Observations on facts, if any, are only for the purpose of disposal of the application for suspension of sentence and shall have no bearing on the merits of the appeal. Certified copy as per rules. (ROHIT ARYA) (SANJEEV S KALGAONKAR) JUDGE JUDGE pd

Reliance on parental testimony alone is inadequate, as demonstrated in the SPL.C/73/2014 case.

Passage: The High Court's ruling in State (Gnct Of Delhi) vs Mr X on 2 April, 2019, serves as a meaningful precedent for cases of child sexual abuse in India. It scrutinizes the initial discharge of the accused, asserting that the mere failure of the victim to report abuse immediately does not invalidate their testimony. The judgment highlights the critical nature of longstanding abuse and the psychological effects it has on child victims, recommending that courts provide special consideration to both the emotional state and the familial context of the accuser in child sexual assault cases. The call for accountability aligns with national child protection laws, pushing for improvements in how such cases are examined in court.
Question: What critical aspects did the High Court address in State (Gnct Of Delhi) vs Mr X that should inform future judicial considerations in cases of child sexual abuse and the associated rights of victims?
Correct Answer: Judicial emphasis on the mental and emotional state of the victim must be a priority, as should the review of familial dynamics impacting reporting.
Reasoning: The judgment reinforces the idea that understanding the psychological impact of trauma and the influence of familial relationships is essential for ensuring justice for child victims in cases of sexual assault.

Passage: Mohd. Aleem, vs The State Oftelangana, on 13 September, 2022. The petitioners claim to be the joint owners of Plot No.64, admeasuring 267 sq. yards situated in Sy.No.844/16 forming part of Annapurna Cooperative Housing Society Limited, Malkajgiri, Ranga Reddy District, having purchased the same under registered sale deed dated 29.09.2010. Subsequently the petitioners constructed Ground plus Three floors (G+3) by obtaining permission from GHMC and the property was assessed with H.No.17-119/A64. The petitioners had been staying in the ground floor of the said building. The petitioners gave first, second and third floor of the building to M/s. Save a Child, a private organization, represented by Sri M. Sunit Kumar, S/o. Christopher, under rental agreement dated 03.07.2014 for a period of 11 months. The lease was extended from time to time. While so, the respondent No.2 addressed a communication dated 22.06.2017, along with a copy of a note approved by the Collector, Medchal-Malkajgiri District, stating that sexual abuse has happened on an inmate of 'Save a Child' orphanage by the founder M. Sunit Kumar and in that connection, the District Collector has given instructions to close and seize the movable and immovable property and bank accounts of the orphanage located in the premises of the petitioners. On the instructions of the District Collector, the respondent No.4 directed the Village Revenue Officer, Malkajgiri, to seize the orphanage, which was being run in the first, second and third floors of the building of the petitioners. Accordingly, three floors were seized on 24.06.2017 under cover of panchanma. Learned counsel for the petitioners submitted that the 'Save a Child' orphanage is the tenant of the petitioners. In the process of taking action against the founder of the orphanage, the property of the petitioners could not have been seized. A request was made by the petitioners vide representation dated 08.10.2017 to the respondent No.3 for releasing the seized property. The petitioners have given the property on lease to the orphanage for a monthly rent of Rs.41,000/-. The petitioner is put to loss since July 2017. There are several maintenance issues in the property. Learned Assistant Government Pleader for Women Development and Child Welfare Department submitted that the orphanage was seized for violation of the provisions of the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015 and the National Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Model Rules, 2016. The petitioners should have checked the registration certificate of the orphanage before giving the property on rent or lease. The petitioners allowed the founder of the orphanage to run a home for children in need of care and protection illegally. The orphanage management did not submit original copies of the rental agreement at the time of inspection. The petitioner did not register the lease deed of the building at the concerned Sub-Registrar. FIR No.446 of 2017 was registered against Sunith Kumar, in-charge of the orphanage, under Section 12 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012 and under Section 376-C and 506 IPC with L.B. Nagar Police Station. The seized movable and immovable property of the orphanage was kept in the building of the petitioners. As per the orders of the I Additional cum Metropolitan Sessions Judge, L.B. Nagar, action will be taken on the movable and immovable properties. Learned counsel for the petitioners submitted that the petitioners are not the accused in FIR.No.446 of 2017. The petitioners are only the owners of the subject premises and the seizure is illegal. As such, there is no requirement for the petitioners to approach the concerned Court for release of the premises/property. It is not in dispute that the petitioners are the owners of the property and the orphanage is the tenant. The contention of the learned Assistant Government Pleader that the petitioners should have registered the lease deed and should have checked the antecedents of the orphanage before giving the property on lease is without any merit. The petitioners, being landlords, would only enquire whether the tenant has the capacity to pay the rents and also ensure that illegal activities are not carried out in the tenanted premises. The misuse of the tenanted property by the tenant and that too after the property was taken on lease cannot in any manner be attributed to the petitioners. The petitioners are not involved in the criminal case, so also the petitioners do not have any civil liability for the illegal activities committed by their tenant and as such, the seizure of the premises is ex facie illegal. However, as the property is stated to have been seized pursuant to registration of FIR.No.446 of 2017, it would be appropriate to dispose of the writ petition with the following directions: 1. The petitioners shall submit an application to the I Additional cum Metropolitan Sessions Judge, L.B. Nagar, seeking release of the subject premises, seized by the Village Revenue Officer, on the directions of the respondent No.4 on 24.06.2017 under the cover of panchanama. 2. The application filed by the petitioners shall be considered and appropriate orders shall be passed forthwith by the I Additional cum Metropolitan Sessions Judge, L.B. Nagar, releasing the subject premises of the petitioners by imposing reasonable conditions. 3. The documents, movable properties, articles etc. of the orphanage viz. Save a Child, shall be forthwith shifted by concerned police with due intimation to the I Additional District Judge cum Metropolitan Sessions Judge, L.B. Nagar and the same shall be kept in the custody of the police station or the Court or any other safe place in accordance with law. If necessary, permission of the Court shall be taken by the police for shifting the documents, movable properties, articles etc. The miscellaneous petitions pending, if any, shall stand closed. There shall be no order as to costs.
Question: What legal remedies are available to the owners of the property based on their claim as joint owners, and how must they proceed given the seizure of their property due to the alleged illegal activities of their tenant's orphanage, which is under investigation for violations of child protection laws and accusations of sexual abuse?
Correct Answer: The owners should immediately apply to the court for the release of their property while ensuring their rights are protected by presenting their case effectively.
Reasoning: The passage states that the petitioners are the legitimate owners of the property and highlights the seizure as illegal, given they were not involved in the criminal activities linked to the tenant. Therefore, the owners can seek judicial intervention to reclaim their property by applying to the court, indicating that they are not responsible for the tenant's actions.

Passage:
Dhanush S/O Suresh vs The State Through on 25 April, 2017
IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA
KALABURAGI BENCH
DATED THIS THE 25TH DAY OF APRIL, 2017
BEFORE
THE HON'BLE MR.JUSTICE B. A. PATIL
CRIMINAL PETITION No.200491/2017
Between:
Dhanush S/o Suresh
Age: 21 years, Occ: Painter
R/o Ashraya Colony, M.G.Road
Kalaburagi
... Petitioner
(By Sri M.S.Kinikeri, Advocate)
And:
The State through
Mahila P.S., Kalaburagi
Represented by Public Prosecutor
High Court Bench, Kalaburagi
... Respondent
(By Sri Prakash Yeli, Addl. SPP.)
This Criminal Petition is filed under Section 439 of Cr.P.C., praying to enlarge the petitioner on bail in Spl. Case NO.12/2017 in Crime No.73/2016 of Mahila Police Station,
Kalaburagi, which is registered for the offences punishable under Sections 363, 376(2)(n) of IPC and Sections 4, 6, 7, 11 and 12 of POCSO Act and also Section 10 of Child Marriage
Restraint Act. Brief facts leading to filing of the complaint are that, on 20.04.2016 at about 7.00 p.m. as the complainant was in her house and there was nobody in the house, petitioner came to the house of the victim, threatened and kidnapped her and thereafter he took her to his uncle's house at Kalaburagi. On 02.05.2016, the sister of the accused Roopa, Mahadevi and uncle Sharanappa all together took her to Nagavi Yallamma temple at Chittapur Taluka and there they solemnized the marriage of the victim with accused forcibly. Thereafter, accused kept the victim girl in his house at Kalaburagi and there he forcibly had sexual intercourse with her and the mother of the accused also helped the accused by way of abusing him to keep her in the house. It is further alleged that accused used to sexually harass her and used to have forcible sexual intercourse with her. With the help of others, she escaped and subsequently on 14.12.2016 she filed a written complaint. On the basis of the said complaint, a case has been registered against the accused persons. I have heard the learned counsel for the petitioner and the learned Additional State Public Prosecutor appearing for respondent-State. The main grounds urged by the learned counsel for the petitioner are that the complaint is baseless and there is a delay in filing the complaint. The said complaint has been filed only with an intention to harass the petitioner. It is also contended that no marriage as alleged in the complaint has been taken place. It is also contended that already charge-sheet has been filed and the remaining accused persons have not been shown as accused. It is further contended that the medical certificate reveals that there is no offence as alleged in the complaint and there are no external injuries found on the victim girl. It is further contended that if the petitioner is detained in custody, it is going to affect his right. Further, it is contended that the petitioner is the permanent resident of Kalaburagi and he is the sole earning member. Further, it is contended that if the petitioner is released on bail, he is ready to abide by the conditions to be imposed by this Court and ready to offer sureties. On these grounds, he prays for allowing the petition. On the contrary, learned Additional State Public Prosecutor appearing for the respondent-State would contend that the petitioner by kidnapping the victim, forcibly got married and thereafter he had sexual assault on a minor girl, who is aged about 16 years. He would also contend that the victim girl has given statement before the Magistrate and the same has been recorded under Section 164 of Cr.P.C. and the learned Magistrate has also been shown as a party to the witness. He would further contend that in the statement of the victim, she has been categorically deposed that the petitioner has threatened and thereafter he had sexually assaulted her. He would further contend that because of the threat and other things the complaint was filed belatedly. At this juncture, if the petitioner is released on bail, he may tamper with the prosecution witnesses, he may abscond and he may not be available for trial. On these grounds, he prays for dismissal of the petition. I have gone through the copy of the complaint and other material produced along with the petition. By going through the contents of the complaint and other material, it indicates that the petitioner had kidnapped the victim on 20.04.2016, thereafter he got married her forcibly on 02.05.2016 and thereafter by threatening her he harassed her sexually and had a sexual intercourse with her. If the medical certificate is perused, it indicates that there is sexual assault has been made on the victim girl. Though the learned counsel for the petitioner would contend that there are no external injuries and other signs of sexual assault, but by going through the statement of the victim recorded under Section 164 of Cr.P.C. and the medical certificate, they corroborate with the sexual assault committed by the petitioner. When the petitioner has committed a sexual assault on a minor girl, under such circumstances, the said offence is considered to be serious and he does not deserve any consideration. As such, the bail petition stands dismissed. However, since the petitioner is in custody, the Trial Court is directed to expedite the matter.
Question: Considering the context of the criminal case filed against Dhanush for his alleged actions involving a minor, which legal protections are afforded to the victim under the POCSO Act and other related laws? Specifically, how do these protections apply in scenarios of forced marriage and sexual assault as described in the passage?
Correct Answer: The POCSO Act provides specific protections to ensure that minors who are victims of sexual offenses are protected and can seek justice through legal channels.
Reasoning: The passage clearly states that Dhanush faces serious allegations under the POCSO Act concerning sexual offenses against a minor. The Act is specifically designed to safeguard children from sexual abuse and exploitation, providing them access to judicial processes and legal representation, hence reinforcing the victim’s rights within this context.

Passage: This Criminal Miscellaneous Petition ... vs Judge on 21 February, 2022. This Criminal Miscellaneous Petition is filed to suspend the sentence imposed against the petitioner in Special S.C.No.5 of 2019, dated 21/02/2022 by the Sessions Judge, Special Court for Exclusive Trial of Cases under POCSO Act, Madurai and enlarge the petitioner on bail pending disposal of the criminal appeal. The case of the prosecution is that the petitioner called the victim girl aged about 9 years very often and sexually abused her by giving 10 rupees or 20 rupees for each occasion and thereafter, threatened the victim girl that in case if she inform the incident to her parents, he would cut her throat. On the basis of the complaint, FIR came to be registered in Crime No.9 of 2018 for the offence under sections 5(m),5(1) and 6 of the protection of Child From Sexual Offices Act, 2012 and subsequently, the case was altered into Sections 5(m), 5(1) and 6 of Protection of Child from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 and Section 506 (i) IPC. After completing investigation, the respondent police has laid the final report for the offences under sections 5(m), 5(1) and 6 of Protection of Child from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 and Section 506(i) IPC as against the petitioner/accused. To prove the prosecution case, 11 witnesses have been examined and 13 documents were marked, apart from that one Court exhibit as Ex.C1. On the side of the accused, one witness, but exhibit was marked. At the conclusion of trial, the trial Court found that the case against the accused was proved, convicted this petitioner and sentenced to undergo rigorous imprisonment for 20 years and to pay a fine of Rs.5,000/- with default clause for the offence under section 5(m), 5(1) and 5(i) r/w 6 of POCSO Act. Challenging the same, the accused has filed the appeal. Pending appeal, this miscellaneous petition is filed seeking suspension of sentence. Heard both sides. Perusal of the records shows that the appellant argued the main appeal before my predecessor and posted for respondent side argument. Now change of roster, the matter has to argue the main appeal, in the meantime, I find no specific ground made by the petitioner in this petition. Accordingly, this criminal miscellaneous petition dismissed. The appellant is directed to argue the main appeal afresh. Index:Yes/No Internet:Yes/No

Passage: In the case of Gundappa vs The State Of Karnataka, the petitioner Gundappa seeks bail under Section 439 of Cr.P.C. related to a crime involving kidnapping and offences under the POCSO Act. Passage: Can criminal proceedings involving non-compoundable sexual offences against women and children be quashed upon a compromise between the accused and the victim, invoking section 482 of the Code of Criminal Procedure? - this is the common issue that falls into consideration in these bunch of cases. The petitioners in all cases are the accused involved in sexual offences either under Chapter XVI of the Indian Penal Code (for short 'IPC') or under Chapter II of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 (for short 'POCSO Act') or both. All of them seek to quash the proceedings on the ground of settlement with the victim. Since there were divergent views on the subject by the Apex Court and High Courts across the country, I have directed the Counsel for the petitioners as well as the Public Prosecutors to address arguments in detail. The learned Counsel for the petitioners submitted that High Court possesses inherent jurisdiction under Section 482 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (for short 'Cr.P.C') to annul an FIR or criminal proceeding in appropriate cases where the offender and the victim have settled their dispute notwithstanding the fact that the offence involved is a sexual offence against women or children. The learned counsel further submitted that when the matter has been amicably settled, the victim would not support the prosecution case, and the possibility of conviction would be bleak. The continuation of proceedings, in such circumstances, would be a sheer waste of precious judicial time and public money, submitted the Counsel. Per contra, the learned Public Prosecutors submitted that rape or sexual offence against women or children is a heinous crime against society, and any compromise between the victim and the offender in relation to such an offence could not ordinarily provide for any basis for quashing the criminal proceedings in exercise of the wholesome power of the High Court under section 482 of Cr.P.C. They further submitted that compromises legalise rape and offer an escape route to the perpetrators. However, they fairly concurred with the view expressed by this court that there cannot be total embargo in exercising the extraordinary power vested with this court under section 482 of Cr.P.C. to quash criminal proceedings based on the settlement between the accused and the sexual assault victim in suitable cases to do complete justice to them.
Question: What legal remedy can the High Court exercise to quash criminal proceedings involving non-compoundable sexual offenses in the context of a settlement between the accused and the victim as presented in this passage?
Correct Answer: It has the power to quash the proceedings based on a settlement in suitable cases to do complete justice to the victim and the accused.
Reasoning: The passage highlights that, despite the seriousness of sexual offences, the High Court possesses the inherent jurisdiction to quash criminal proceedings if the offender and victim have settled their dispute. Moreover, it mentions the court's ability to exercise its power under section 482 of the Cr.P.C. to do justice based on the settlement, indicating the possibility of quashing such proceedings in suitable cases.

Both the learned advocates would submit that during the pendency of present petition, the matter is amicably settled amongst the parties and therefore, any further continuation of the proceedings pursuant to the impugned FIR would create hardship to the parties and further continuation of the proceedings would amount to abuse of process of law. 6. Learned APP has strongly opposed this application. 7. The complainant - respondent No. 2 - Navalsinh Motibhai Minama as well as the prosecutrix have filed affidavits stating inter-alia the fact that the matter is amicably settled with the applicant which are ordered to be taken on record and therefore, in the interest of justice, FIR may be quashed and set aside. 8. Having heard the learned counsel for the parties and considering the facts of settlement and law laid down by the Apex Court [Gian Singh Vs. State of Punjab & Anr., reported in (2012) 10 SCC 303, Madan Mohan Abbot Vs. State of Punjab, reported in (2008) 4 SCC 582, Nikhil Merchant Vs. Central Bureau of Investigation & Anr., reported in 2009 (1) GLH 31, Manoj Sharma Vs. State & Ors., reported in 2009 (1) GLH 190 and Narinder Singh & Ors. Vs. State of Punjab & Anr. reported in 2014 (2) Crime 67 (SC),] this Court is of the considered view that further continuation of the criminal proceedings in relation to the impugned FIR would nothing but unnecessary harassment to the parties and trial thereon would be futile and further continuation of the proceedings would amount to abuse of process of law. Thus, to secure the ends of justice, the impugned FIR is required to be quashed and set aside in exercise of powers conferred under Section 482 of the Code. 9. Resultantly, this application is allowed and the impugned FIR bearing C.R.No.I-73 of 2017 registered with Chhani Police Station, Dist.- Vadodara filed against present applicant is hereby quashed and set aside and all other proceedings arising out of the aforesaid FIR are also quashed and set aside. Direct service is permitted.
Question: In light of the decision to quash the FIR related to serious offences against a minor, what kind of support mechanisms should be provided for the victim to ensure their well-being after such a judicial decision, especially in terms of psychological and legal support?
Correct Answer: Providing counseling and access to legal aid and child protective services is essential to support the victim post-decision.
Reasoning: The passage highlights the quashing of the FIR, indicating that despite legal proceedings ending, the welfare of the victim remains critical. Support mechanisms such as counseling and access to legal resources are vital to help victims navigate their emotional, psychological, and legal needs following a decision of this nature.

Passage: K.Vijay vs The State on 29 November, 2022. This Criminal Original Petition has been preferred to call for the records in Spl.SC.No.27 of 2020 on the file of the Principal District Judge cum Special Judge (Under POCSO Act) Puducherry and quash the same. The first petitioner is the sole accused in Crime No.92 of 2019 on the file of the first respondent police. The second petitioner is the wife of the first petitioner. The Child Welfare Committee, Puducherry, has given the complaint to the first respondent that the first petitioner had committed sexual offences on the second petitioner. The said case came to be registered when the second petitioner who was conceived then, went to Rajiv Gandhi Government Hospital, Puducherry for medical check up. The second petitioner was married to the first petitioner as early as on 27.04.2019 and they started to live as a family at the first petitioner's house at Puducherry. When the second petitioner got pregnant, she was taking treatment at Rajiv Gandhi Government Hospital, Puducherry. During the medical examination, she revealed her age that she was 16 years old and that prompted the Hospital authorities to pass information to the second respondent Child Welfare Committee and the second respondent, in turn, had given a complaint to the first respondent Police on the above allegation; and on which, FIR has been registered under Section 6 of Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 after investigation, final report was filed and the same has been taken on file in Spl.SC.No.27 of 2020 for the offences under Section 361 IPC read with Section 6 of Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 and Section 9 of Prohibition of Child Marriage Act, 2006, on the file of the Principal District Judge cum Special Judge (Under POCSO Act) Puducherry. When the matter is taken up for hearing, both the petitioners present before this Court and filed their respective affidavits dated 10.11.2022 stating that the relationship between them is out of their marriage that had been taken place in the year 2019 and their marital life cannot be taken as a criminal offence. At no point of time, the second petitioner had intended to give any complaint against the first petitioner. Because she considers the first petitioner as her husband and for whom, she had delivered one female child. Now, the second petitioner is pregnant for her second child and the matter came to be known only when she had been to the hospital for her routine medical check up. Further, both the petitioners have also filed their respective affidavits dated 10.11.2022 stating that the continuation of the prosecution would only spoil their peaceful family life and also the future of their daughter. When enquired, both the petitioners submitted that they fell in love with each other and hence, they got married and their marital life is happy and peaceful. No useful purpose is going to be served by subjecting the first petitioner under trial. Even though the offence is non-compoundable, in the extraneous circumstances of the present case, I feel it is appropriate to rely on the principles laid down by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in the case of PARBATBHAI AAHIR VS. STATE OF GUJARAT [AIR 2017 SC 4843] to quash the proceedings. In the said case, in Paragraph 15, it has held as under: 15. The Broad Principles which emerge from the precedents on the subject, may be summarised in the following propositions: (i) Section 482 Cr.P.C preserves the inherent powers of the High Court to prevent an abuse of the process of any court or to secure the ends of justice. The provision does not confer new powers. It only recognises and preserves powers which inherent in the High Court. (ii) The invocation of the jurisdiction of the High Court to quash a first information report or a criminal proceeding on the ground that a settlement has been arrived at between the offender and the victim is not the same as the invocation of jurisdiction for the purpose of compounding an offence. While compounding an offence, the power of the court is governed by the provisions of Section 320 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973. The power to quash under Section 482 is attracted even if the offence is non-compoundable. In the result, this Criminal Original Petition is allowed and as a sequel, the further proceedings in Spl.SC.No.27 of 2020 on the file of the learned Principal District Judge Cum Special Judge (Under POCSO Act) Puducherry is quashed.
Question: In light of the events described in the case of K.Vijay vs The State, discuss the implications of the second petitioner's age and her relationship with the first petitioner on the judicial process and outcomes related to the POCSO Act. How might these factors influence both the prosecution and the defense in this legal matter?
Correct Answer: The second petitioner's age places her within a category requiring protective laws that could complicate prosecutorial decisions.
Reasoning: The fact that the second petitioner was 16 at the time of the alleged crimes necessitates the invocation of the protective measures of the POCSO Act, revealing the complexities inherent in prosecuting cases involving minors, especially in the context of familial or marital ties. This complicates the prosecution's position while giving the defense leverage based on the relationship.

Passage: X vs State Of Nct Of Delhi (Acting Through Its ... on 20 October, 2022. The present case raises important questions regarding the child survivors of sexual abuse. Child sexual abuse (hereinafter called "CSA") has been interpreted by the World Health Organisation (hereinafter called "WHO") as the involvement of a child in sexual activity that he or she does not fully comprehend, is unable to give informed consent to, or for which the child is not developmentally prepared and cannot give consent, or that violates the laws or social taboos of society. In a study, it was found that about 37% of India's population comprises children under 18, while around 53% of Indian children reported experiencing different kinds of abuse. The impact of the sexual offence is rightly pointed out in Bodhisattwa Gautam v. Subhra Chakraborty, wherein the Supreme Court reiterated that "Rape is not only a crime against the person of a woman (victim), it is a crime against the entire society. It destroys the entire psychology of a woman and pushed her into deep emotional crises..." Owing to various emotional, social, and cultural factors, CSA survivors may not be able to express their feelings and experiences. The main rationale for this is that children are traumatized and are unsure of the proper words to express their encounter. CSA has long-lasting negative effects on mental health. The criminal claims the survivors life, if not physically then affecting their mental and emotional state. It, thus, becomes imperative that the survivor of the crime is not overlooked and rehabilitation for the sufferings of the survivor is not overlooked. There is duty cast on the State to protect their rights and deliver justice to the survivors. This justice, in the form of compensation, should be given its liberal meaning thereby giving the maximum benefit and as quickly as possible. The Law Commission’s 154th Report (1996) on reforms in criminal procedure discussed concerns related to ‘victimology.’ A whole branch has evolved to make the victim the focus of their study. The tools employed by this study include an understanding of the victim-offender relationship, assessing, and possible ways for compensating the victims.
Question: What steps should the State take to ensure adequate support and compensation for child survivors of sexual abuse according to the passage? What mechanisms are suggested for the rehabilitation of child victims, particularly considering their emotional and psychological traumas? Discuss the implications of justice as mentioned in the passage and how these steps impact the victims' rehabilitation.
Correct Answer: The State should recognize the long-term effects of sexual abuse and offer policies to comprehensively support child survivors.
Reasoning: The passage emphasizes the need for supportive systems including rehabilitation and compensation frameworks that take into account the ongoing trauma experienced by child survivors. It stresses the importance of immediate and adequate responses from the State to support the victims' rights and well-being.

Passage: SC No. 41/17 State vs . Dilkhus on 5 June, 2017 IN THE COURT OF MS. SEEMA MAINI ADDITIONAL SESSIONS JUDGE­01 : NORTH ROHINI : NEW DELHI. In the matter of:­ (Sessions Case No. 41/17) Unique Identification No. DLNT01­000790­2016 FIR No. 538/2016 Police Station Narela Under Section 354 A/506 IPC & 8 POCSO Act State V/s Dilkhus S/o Israfil R/o Village Chietjor, PS Kotoriya District Banka, Bihar. Presently R/o Rinku Ka Makan Kureni, Narela, Delhi­110058 ......Accused Date of institution 19.01.2017 Date of arguments 05.06.2017 Judgment Pronounced on 05.06.2017 Decision Acquitted Judgment : FIR No. 538/16 page 1 of 9SC No. 41/17 State Vs. Dilkhus The accused Dilkhus is facing trial in the present case on allegations of having made physical contact and advances involving unwelcome and explicit sexual overtures and threatening the victim / prosecutrix 'K' (identity withheld), aged about 12 years. The facts in brief, which are borne out from the record are that on 22.07.2016, a complaint was lodged by the complainant Mr. 'S' father of the victim 'K' (identity withheld) in PS Narela to the effect that the accused Dilkhus, who was living in his house as tenant, and was aged about 19 years, for the past many days, had been making obscene and vulgar gestures and had been harassing his 12 years old daughter i.e. victim K. On 22.07.2016 at about 7.00 AM, victim K had returned from her school due to ill health, and the complainant Mr. S alongwith his wife, was taking the victim K to the hospital for her checkup, and the victim K was walking 5­7 steps behind them. At that time, the accused came and caught hold of the hand of the victim K and also started harassing her and even stated that he loved her. When the victim K shouted and informed the complainant about the activities of the accused, he (accused) threatened them with life. Immediately the complainant ran and apprehended the accused, and with the help of other public persons, gave him beatings. During this time, someone called the police at 100 Number. Police arrived at the spot of occurrence, where the statement of the complainant was recorded, on the basis of which, Rukka was prepared and was sent to the PS through constable, who was accompanying the IO, for registration of the FIR. On the basis of the Rukka, the instant FIR bearing No. 538/16 was registered. The victim was medically examined at SRHC Hospital, where she refused to get her internal medical examination done. Accused was also got medically examined. On the pointing out of the complainant, the site plan of the spot of occurrence was prepared, and accused was arrested vide his arrest memo and personal search memo, statements of the witnesses were recorded u/s 161 CrPC. Statement of the victim K was also got recorded u/s 164 CrPC, and after the conclusion of the investigation, charge­sheet was filed in the court.
Question: In the context of the passage detailing the legal proceedings against Dilkhus, what steps should have been taken by the police to ensure the protection of the child victim K during the investigation, given her age and the nature of the accusations?
Correct Answer: The police should ensure the child's safety and well-being, involve child protection services, and proceed sensitively with the investigation.
Reasoning: Since the victim K is a minor, it is legally imperative for the police to prioritize her safety and mental well-being during the investigation. The POCSO Act mandates that cases involving child victims must be handled sensitively, and child protection services should be involved to protect the child's interests.

Passage: Sreesh P.T vs The State Of Kerala on 17 January, 2019. Bail Appl..No. 244 of 2019. Crime No. 350/2018 of Thottipalam Police Station, Kozhikode. This application is filed under § 439 of the Cr.P.C. The applicant claims to be a teenager. He finds himself arrayed as the sole accused in Crime No.350 of 2018 of the Thottilpalam Police Station. In the aforesaid case, he faces accusations of having committed offence punishable under Sections 354, 363 & 376 of the IPC and Section 7 r/w. Section 8 and Section 3 r/w. Section 4 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012. According to the prosecution, on 8.10.2018, the police was alerted that a minor child was found in the house of the applicant under suspicious circumstances. They reached the spot and questioned the child. She disclosed about her whereabouts and on the strength of the said information, her mother was informed. Based on the statement of the mother, the Crime was initially registered under Sections 363 and 354 and the relevant provisions of the POCSO Act, 2012. On further questioning, she is alleged to have disclosed that she was in a relationship with the applicant herein and on an earlier occasion, she had gone to the house of the applicant near Semitherimukku and he had subjected her to penetrative sexual intercourse. Section 376 of the IPC and graver provisions of the POCSO Act were later incorporated. The learned counsel appearing for the applicant would contend that the applicant is innocent. According to the learned counsel, the applicant was just 19 years old when the offence was committed. The allegation of sexual abuse levelled against him is not true, submits the learned counsel. He is from a poor background, whereas the parents of the victim are affluent. He would finally submit that the applicant, a teenager, has been housed in a jail with hardened criminals for over three months and his association with such men would only aid in transforming him to a criminal. Heard the learned Public Prosecutor, who strenuously opposed the prayer. It is submitted that the applicant herein had escaped from the police custody and thereafter, he was re-arrested on 17.10.2018. Crime No.361 of 2018 has also been registered inter alia under Sections 353 and 332 of the IPC as against the applicant. The learned Public Prosecutor would also contend that the allegations are extremely grave. However, it is fairly submitted that after completion of the investigation, final reports have already been laid in both the crimes. I have considered the submissions advanced and have gone through the materials made available. Other than these two crimes, the applicant is not involved in any other crimes. Records would show that he has been in custody from 08.10.2018 itself. The final report has already been laid as well. In that view of the matter, I do not think that detaining the applicant for a further period would serve any purpose. By imposing appropriate conditions, for safeguarding the interest of the prosecution as well as the protection of the victim, the applicant can now be released. In the result, this application will stand allowed. The applicant shall be released on bail on his executing a bond for Rs.1,00,000/-(Rupees One lakh only) with two solvent sureties each for the like sum to the satisfaction of the court having jurisdiction. The above order shall be subject to the following conditions: 1). The applicant shall appear before the Investigating Officer on all Saturdays between 10 a.m. and 1.00 p.m., for a period of 3 months. 2). He shall not enter the limits of Thottilpalam Police Station for a period of six months except for complying with the conditions of this order. If for any extraordinary reason the applicant requires to enter the limits, previous permission has to be obtained from the jurisdictional Court. 3). He shall not intimidate or attempt to influence the witnesses; nor shall he tamper with the evidence. He shall not contact the victim or her family members. 4). He shall not commit any offence while he is on bail. 5). He shall not leave India without the permission of the Court and if having passport, shall deposit the same before the Trial Court within a week; If release of the passport is required at a later period, he shall be at liberty to move appropriate application before the Court having jurisdiction. In case of violation of any of the above conditions, the jurisdictional Court shall be empowered to consider the application for cancellation, if any, and pass appropriate orders in accordance with the law.
Question: What specific protective measures does the court take in order to safeguard the minor child victim during the legal proceedings involving Sreesh P.T as per the passage provided?
Correct Answer: The court imposes conditions on the accused to prevent him from contacting the victim or tampering with evidence.
Reasoning: The passage indicates that the court set clear conditions that prohibit the accused from contacting the victim or her family, showing the court's awareness of the need for protective measures during the legal process.

Passage: The petitioner, who is aged about 18 years and 2 months, is alleged to be the victim of the crime in Crime No.376 of 2021, which was registered, on the basis of the complaint given by the 2nd respondent herein for the offences punishable under Sections 8, 11(1), 12, 17 and 21(2) of the Protection of Child from Sexual Offences Act, 2012.
Question: What protections are afforded to victims under the POCSO Act as implied in the passage, particularly regarding false allegations and the rights of the victim as they navigate through legal proceedings?
Correct Answer: Victims can appeal against false allegations and seek legal protection to safeguard their rights.
Reasoning: The passage emphasizes that the petitioner is a victim of a crime and implies that the POCSO Act is designed to protect victims, including their rights to respond against false allegations and provide for their legal safety throughout proceedings.
